title: Missing unhandledRejection and signal handlers leave terminal in broken state on crash
description: In index.tsx lines 31-37, only `uncaughtException` is handled. Missing: (1) `unhandledRejection` — a rejected promise (e.g. from the AI client) will crash the process without restoring the terminal cursor or alternate screen. (2) `SIGINT`/`SIGTERM` — if the user hits Ctrl+C or the process is killed, the terminal title remains set and cursor may stay hidden. The uncaughtException handler correctly writes escape sequences to restore terminal state, but these other exit paths skip that cleanup entirely.
notes: File: src/index.tsx lines 31-37. Add: process.on('unhandledRejection', ...) with same terminal restore logic, and process.on('SIGINT'/'SIGTERM', ...) that restores terminal state before exiting. Also consider clearing the terminal title (ESC ]0; ESC \) in the cleanup sequence.
close_reason: Signal handlers merged. Commit 5a54e8e.

title: Hardcoded color 'yellow' in TerminalGuard bypasses COLORS constant
description:
In `src/lib/terminal.tsx` line 50, the TerminalGuard component uses the raw Ink color name `"yellow"` instead of a COLORS constant:

```tsx
<Text color="yellow">
  Terminal too narrow (need {MIN_WIDTH}+ cols, currently {columns})
</Text>
```

Every other color usage in the codebase goes through `COLORS.*` from `src/constants.ts`. This is the only instance of a bare color string.

**Fix:** Replace `color="yellow"` with `color={COLORS.amber}` (the closest palette color to yellow) and add the COLORS import. This maintains the warning tone while staying on-palette.
close_reason: Fixed bare yellow to COLORS.amber. Commit 14e2614.

title: Mission 09: git diff question teaches wrong muscle
description: The command step asking 'What command would you ask Claude Code to run to see unstaged changes?' expects 'git diff'. This contradicts the core lesson — Claude Code is agentic, you describe outcomes not commands. Users should learn to say 'show me what changed' not memorize git commands. Replace with a quiz that tests understanding of the natural-language workflow.
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum.ts

title: App force-exits without API key instead of running in offline mode
close_reason: Fixed: always render App, pass hasApiKey for graceful degradation

title: Player gets no feedback on WHICH questions they got wrong during a mission
description:
During mission gameplay, when a player gets a question wrong they see 'COMPROMISED' + the correct answer + explanation, then the mission immediately advances to the next step. There is no way to review wrong answers after the mission completes.

The Debrief screen shows stars, FXP, cover integrity, and cosmetic objective checkmarks — but NOT which specific questions the player got wrong. The MissionState type tracks 'answers: Answer[]' with stepIndex and correct boolean, but this data is not surfaced anywhere in the UI.

For a learning tool, this is a critical gap. A player who got 2 out of 5 questions wrong needs to know WHICH 2, so they can study those topics. Without this, the only option is to replay the entire mission and hope to encounter the same questions (which they will, since the curriculum is static).

The debrief is the perfect place for this — a 'Review your answers' section showing each question, the player's answer, and whether it was correct.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a 'Mission Review' section to the Debrief screen (or a separate screen accessible from Debrief)
- Show each quiz/command question, whether the player answered correctly, and the correct answer if they got it wrong
- Include the explanation text for wrong answers so the player can learn
- This data already exists in MissionState.answers — it just needs to be surfaced
close_reason: Fixed — Debrief now shows which questions were answered wrong

title: Credits screen uses amber border while all other main screens use cyan
description:
The Credits screen at src/screens/Credits.tsx line 63-64 uses:

```tsx
borderStyle="double"
borderColor={COLORS.amber}
```

Every other main screen with a double border uses `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`:
- Logo.tsx:49 — `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`
- Briefing.tsx:59 — `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`
- MissionMap.tsx:94 — `borderColor={primary}` (which is `COLORS.cyan` in normal mode)
- Debrief.tsx:75 — `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`
- InfiniteMode.tsx:247 — `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}`

The Credits screen is the sole outlier. Its header text is `[ DECLASSIFIED ]` in red, which already communicates the special/secret nature. The amber border breaks the visual system without adding meaning.

**Fix:** Change `borderColor={COLORS.amber}` to `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}` on the Credits screen to match the established pattern. The red header and self-destruct animation already make it feel unique.
close_reason: Credits border changed to cyan. Commit 14e2614.

title: InfiniteMode confirm phase label says 'Tier' while selection phase says 'clearance tier'
description:
In InfiniteMode, the difficulty selection uses different labels across phases:

**difficulty-select phase (line 298):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan}>Select clearance tier:</Text>
```

**confirm phase (line 317-321):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan}>
  Tier:{" "}
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    {selectedDifficulty?.toUpperCase()}
  </Text>
</Text>
```

The selection prompt says "clearance tier" but the confirmation shows just "Tier". The DIFFICULTY_TIERS constant (lines 28-43) has labels like "RECRUIT - Recognition-level recall" which use the clearance level names.

Also compare with the topic display across phases:
- topic-select: "Select mission topic for field assessment:" (line 278)
- difficulty-select: "Topic: {selectedTopic}" (line 293-296)
- confirm: "Topic: {selectedTopic}" (line 311-315)

Topic is consistent ("Topic:") but difficulty changes label from "clearance tier" to "Tier".

**Fix:** Change the confirm phase label from "Tier:" to "Clearance:" to match the Briefing screen pattern (Briefing.tsx:40 shows \`CLEARANCE: RECRUIT\`). Or use "Clearance tier:" for both the prompt and the confirmation.
close_reason: InfiniteMode tier label standardized. Commit afa3295.

title: Build broken: Mission.tsx:169 syntax error — extra } in useInput call
description: The useInput call at Mission.tsx:152-169 has an extraneous closing brace on line 169. The pattern `});` should be `);` since the arrow function body is already closed on line 168. Both tsc and esbuild catch this: `error TS1005: ',' expected` and `Expected ")" but found "}"`. This cascades to fail 4 test files (e2e/full-app, integration/app-flow, integration/mission-flow, screens/Mission) that import Mission.tsx.
close_reason: Fixed: removed extraneous } from Mission.tsx:169 useInput call. Merged to main.

title: Unguarded setTimeout calls can fire after component unmount
description: Two components use setTimeout without cleanup, which can cause React state updates on unmounted components: (1) CommandStep.tsx line 92 — setTimeout inside handleSubmit callback fires onAnswer after a delay but is never cleared if the component unmounts during the delay. (2) MissionMap.tsx line 53 — setTimeout for konamiFlash reset (2000ms) is never cleaned up if the user navigates away from MissionMap before the timeout fires. Both can cause 'setState on unmounted component' warnings or subtle state corruption.
notes: Files: src/components/CommandStep.tsx line 92, src/screens/MissionMap.tsx line 53. Fix: store the timeout ID in a ref and clear it in a useEffect cleanup. For CommandStep, the setTimeout is inside a useCallback — move the timer tracking to a ref that gets cleared in a useEffect return.
close_reason: setTimeout leak fix merged. Refs + cleanup for CommandStep and MissionMap. Commit 22f57f8.

title: Cover blown screen lacks visual weight compared to other error/alert states
description:
The "cover blown" state (src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 180-194) is the most dramatic moment in the game — total mission failure — but it's visually understated compared to other alerts:

**Current cover blown display:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" gap={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.red} bold>
    ██  COVER BLOWN  ██
  </Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.red}>
    Your identity has been compromised. Mission failed.
  </Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.gray}>
    FXP earned this attempt will be retained.
  </Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    [ENTER] Restart mission
  </Text>
</Box>
```

Compare with other prominent UI moments:
- **Achievement notification**: double border in gold, centered, with slide animation
- **NoApiKey warning**: double border in red, centered, with structured layout
- **Quit confirmation**: single border in amber, centered overlay

The cover blown state is just flat text in the content area with no border, no centering, no visual container. For the app's most punishing moment, this feels anticlimactic. The "\u2588\u2588  COVER BLOWN  \u2588\u2588" text with block characters is creative but gets lost without a visual frame.

**Fix:** Wrap the cover blown content in a bordered container to give it visual weight:
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" alignItems="center" justifyContent="center" flexGrow={1}>
  <Box borderStyle="double" borderColor={COLORS.red} paddingX={2} paddingY={1} flexDirection="column">
    <Box justifyContent="center" marginBottom={1}>
      <Text color={COLORS.red} bold>[ COVER BLOWN ]</Text>
    </Box>
    <Text color={COLORS.red}>Your identity has been compromised. Mission failed.</Text>
    <Text color={COLORS.gray}>FXP earned this attempt will be retained.</Text>
    <Box marginTop={1}>
      <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>[ENTER] Restart mission</Text>
    </Box>
  </Box>
</Box>
```
This uses the red border treatment (matching NoApiKey's warning style) and the bracketed header convention.
close_reason: Cover blown bordered container. Commit 6f42bed.

title: NoApiKey cost estimate '/bin/bash.05-0.15 per mission' contradicts README '/bin/bash.15 total for all 12'
description: The NoApiKey screen (src/screens/NoApiKey.tsx line 67) says 'Estimated cost: ~$0.05-0.15 per mission (Haiku-class models)'. The README (line 161) says 'A full 12-mission playthrough typically costs under $0.15.' If the NoApiKey screen's estimate is right, the total cost would be $0.60-1.80. If the README is right, the per-mission cost is about $0.01. These cannot both be correct. The README estimate is likely more accurate since most missions are static (no API calls for quiz/print steps). The NoApiKey screen should say something like 'Estimated total cost: ~$0.15 for the full curriculum' to match the README.
close_reason: Updated NoApiKey screen cost text to match README: '~/bin/bash.15 for the full curriculum'

title: No progress indicator during typewriter animation — player does not know how long to wait
description:
During print steps, the player watches text appear character by character with no indication of how long it will take or how much text remains. The StatusBar shows 'Step X/Y' which tells you how many steps are left in the mission, but not how long the current text animation will take.

For short print steps this is fine. For longer ones (200-300 characters at dramatic speed = 8-12 seconds), the player has no way to know if the animation is 20% done or 80% done. They just watch and wait.

This is especially frustrating because:
1. There is no skip mechanism (separate issue)
2. The player cannot tell if the game is frozen or just slow
3. Dramatic speed (40ms/char) on a long paragraph feels like it might be stuck

Even a simple progress bar or character count would help orient the player.
acceptance_criteria:
- This is lower priority if typewriter skip is implemented (since players can just skip)
- If implemented: a subtle progress indicator (percentage, dots, or a thin progress bar) during long typewriter animations
- Should be unobtrusive — the typewriter effect is the main experience, the indicator is just reassurance
- Only show for animations expected to take >5 seconds
close_reason: Fixed — eval timeout with fallback + step progress indicator

title: Unit tests: progress store module (store/progress.ts)
description:
Test file: `__tests__/store/progress.test.ts`

Module under test: `src/store/progress.ts`

## Test cases

### loadProgress
- Returns default SaveData on first load (all zeroed/empty)
- Returns previously saved data after saveProgress is called
- Returns copy (not reference) to prevent external mutation

### saveProgress
- Persists full SaveData object
- Overwrites previous data completely

### saveMissionComplete
- Adds mission ID to completedMissions
- Does not duplicate mission ID if already completed
- Sets star rating for mission
- Only upgrades star rating (never downgrades -- e.g., 3 stars stays if new is 2)
- Accumulates FXP (adds to existing total)
- Clears partial progress for the mission

### saveStepProgress / getPartialProgress
- saveStepProgress stores step index for mission ID
- getPartialProgress returns stored step index
- getPartialProgress returns null for unknown mission ID
- Multiple missions can have independent partial progress
- saveStepProgress overwrites previous value for same mission

### resetProgress
- Resets all data to DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA
- Clears partial progress
- loadProgress returns defaults after reset

### unlockAchievement
- Returns true on first unlock of an achievement
- Returns false on duplicate unlock attempt
- Adds achievement ID to achievements array
- Does not duplicate achievement IDs

### markHandlerUsed
- Sets handlerEverUsed to true
- Is idempotent (calling twice does not error)

### updateLastPlayed
- Sets lastPlayedAt to current timestamp
- Updates on subsequent calls

### saveInfiniteResult
- Accumulates correct count
- Accumulates total count
- Increments sessionsPlayed
- Accumulates fxpEarned in stats
- Also adds fxpEarned to global fxp total

### reportBadQuestion
- Writes JSONL entry to ~/.claude-code-academy/reported-questions.jsonl
- Does not throw on filesystem errors (best-effort)

### toggleLegacyMode
- Toggles legacyModeUnlocked from false to true
- Toggles back from true to false
- Returns the new value

### hasSaveData
- Returns false for default data
- Returns true when completedMissions is non-empty
- Returns true when fxp > 0
- Returns true when firstRunComplete is true

## Mocking strategy
- Mock the `conf` package to avoid writing to real filesystem
- OR use the memory fallback by making createConf fail
- Mock fs operations for reportBadQuestion
- Use vi.spyOn(Date, 'now') for updateLastPlayed tests
- Reset module state between tests (the module has module-level singletons)

## Important notes
- The module has an in-memory fallback (memoryStore) -- test both paths if possible
- Module-level state (partialProgress, memoryStore, usingMemory) must be reset between tests
- Consider using vi.resetModules() and dynamic imports to get fresh module state per test
close_reason: Merged, tests passing

title: Mission 10: /init command question is unfair — /init never mentioned in preceding text
description:
Mission 10 (LEGEND BUILDER) has a command step asking: 'What command initializes a new CLAUDE.md in your project by analyzing the codebase?' Expected answer: '/init'.

The problem: the preceding print steps in Mission 10 discuss CLAUDE.md structure, layering user/project files, and coding conventions — but never mention the /init command. The player has no way to know the answer unless they already know Claude Code.

Compare with Mission 05 which properly teaches /cost and /clear in a print step BEFORE asking about them. Mission 10 skips the teaching step for /init and jumps straight to testing.

Mission 05 (SIGNAL CHAIN) does mention /init would not be known from there either — it lists /help, /cost, /compact, /clear, /config, /doctor but NOT /init.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a print step before the /init command question that introduces the /init command and explains what it does
- OR convert this to a multiple-choice question where /init is one of the options (so the player can reason about it)
- The curriculum should never test knowledge it has not yet taught
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum.ts

title: InfiniteMode 'Report bad question' logs to filesystem with no user feedback on success
description: When a player presses 'R' to report a bad question in InfiniteMode (line 203-217), it calls reportBadQuestion which writes to ~/.claude-code-academy/reported-questions.jsonl. The UI shows '[R] Reported' in green (line 379-380) which is good feedback. However, reportBadQuestion (store/progress.ts line 176-193) silently catches errors. If the write fails (permissions, disk full, etc.), the player sees '[R] Reported' but nothing was actually saved. Should bubble up the error to show '[R] Report failed' or at minimum log it.

title: Mission 09 has an AI step that renders as '[AI Step — coming soon]' placeholder
description:
Mission 09 (DOUBLE AGENT) step index 8 is an AI step with prompt 'Give me an example prompt I could send to Claude Code to start a TDD workflow...' — but AI steps render as a gray placeholder: '[AI Step — coming soon] {prompt text}'.

This happens in Mission.tsx renderStep() which just shows a Text component for type 'ai'. The player sees placeholder text in the middle of their mission, which breaks immersion and feels like a broken feature.

This also affects Mission 10 (step index 7), Mission 11 (step index 7), and Mission 12 (step index 8) — four AI steps total across the curriculum.
design: Short-term fix: replace these 4 AI steps with print steps that contain a pre-written example response to the prompt. This unblocks the full curriculum without requiring AI integration. Long-term: implement AI steps properly with the evaluator or a generator model.
acceptance_criteria:
- AI steps should either be fully implemented with actual AI interaction OR removed/replaced with quiz/print steps until the feature is ready
- If kept as placeholders, they should be visually distinct and clearly communicated as 'coming soon' rather than looking like a rendering bug
- No placeholder text should appear during normal mission gameplay
close_reason: Fixed by cca-f2q — AI steps now render as TypeWriter with onComplete instead of placeholder

title: Infinite Mode InfiniteMode quiz answer handler ignores null from eval failure
description: The InfiniteMode component (src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx) passes handleAnswer to QuizStep (line 372-376), but handleAnswer only accepts (isCorrect: boolean). The QuizStep's onAnswer prop type is (correct: boolean) => void. However, in the regular Mission flow, the quiz/command steps can pass null to indicate eval failure (Mission.tsx handleAnswer line 133). If the evaluator ever fails in Infinite Mode, the behavior is undefined — QuizStep never passes null, so this is only relevant if someone changes QuizStep to pass null like CommandStep. But the inconsistency between Mission's handleAnswer (accepts boolean|null) and InfiniteMode's handleAnswer (accepts boolean only) could cause subtle bugs if the step component contracts change.
close_reason: Not a bug: QuizStep.onAnswer has type (correct: boolean) => void, never passes null. Only CommandStep.onAnswer uses boolean|null. InfiniteMode uses QuizStep exclusively, so null is never an input.

title: Command step reveal mode: self-assessment when no API key
description: When hasApiKey is false, command steps should: 1) Still show text input for recall-based learning, 2) Try localMatch first, 3) On localMatch failure, show expected answer and ask 'Did your answer match? [Y/N]' instead of marking wrong, 4) Trust self-assessment for scoring. Spy framing: 'Comms are down, agent. Verify your own intel.'
close_reason: Implemented self-assessment mode for command steps when hasApiKey=false. LocalMatch tried first; on failure, shows expected answer with Y/N self-assessment prompt. 6 new tests added.

title: Build screen router and state management
description:
Core app architecture: screen state + overlay state + keyboard routing.

Screen state enum: 'logo' | 'onboarding' | 'missionMap' | 'briefing' | 'mission' | 'debrief'
Overlay state: { handler: boolean, help: boolean } — independent of screen

src/hooks/useScreenState.ts:
- Manages screen transitions
- Manages overlay open/close
- Screen transitions trigger ScreenTransition animation

src/app.tsx keyboard routing via useInput():
- ? opens handler overlay (if in mission and AI available)
- ESC closes overlay if open, otherwise opens mission map
- h opens help overlay (keybinding reference)
- q on mission map prompts exit confirmation
- During quiz: q types the letter (input goes to focused component, not router)

Focus management:
- When handler overlay opens, it owns focus
- When overlay closes, focus returns to mission step
- useFocus/useFocusManager throughout (REQUIRED for ink-select-input + ink-text-input coexistence)

Acceptance: All screen transitions work, overlays layer correctly, keyboard routing correct

title: Bug: Debrief shows stale cover integrity when last step is a wrong answer
description: In src/screens/Mission.tsx, when the last interactive step is answered incorrectly, handleAnswer() calls setCoverIntegrity(newCover) then immediately calls advanceStep(). Since advanceStep is a useCallback that closes over the coverIntegrity state variable, it still sees the pre-decrement value. The onComplete callback (line 118) passes this stale coverIntegrity to the Debrief screen, which displays one more cover block than the player actually has. For example, if the player enters the last step with cover=3, answers wrong (should become 2), the Debrief shows 3 blocks instead of 2. Stars are correct because hitsRef is used. Fix: use a ref for coverIntegrity in onComplete (like hitsRef and fxpEarnedRef), or compute coverRemaining from hitsRef.current: coverRemaining = MAX_COVER - hitsRef.current.
close_reason: Fixed: derive coverRemaining from MAX_COVER - hitsRef.current instead of stale closure

title: evaluateAnswer localFallback has inconsistent return shape vs AI evaluation path
description: In evaluator.ts, the localFallback function (used when AI is unavailable) returns a hardcoded object shape that may drift from the zod-validated AI response shape over time. The AI path validates through evaluationSchema (json-utils.ts) which enforces {correct, confidence, feedback}, but the local fallback constructs the same shape manually without validation. If the schema ever changes, the fallback will silently return the wrong shape.
notes: File: src/ai/evaluator.ts. The localFallback at the bottom of evaluateAnswer returns {correct: false, confidence: 'low', feedback: '...'}. Fix: run the fallback result through the same evaluationSchema.parse() to ensure consistency, or extract a createEvaluationResult() factory function used by both paths.

title: Mission 02 teaches npm-based install (Node.js) but text says 'no Node.js needed' — potential factual error
description:
Mission 02 (DEAD DROP) says: 'System requirements are light. Claude Code runs natively on macOS, Linux, and Windows — no Node.js or other runtime needed.'

It then teaches a native installer: 'curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash'

And a quiz asks: 'What external runtime does the native Claude Code installer require?' Correct answer: 'None — it installs with zero dependencies.'

HOWEVER: As of the knowledge cutoff, Claude Code historically required Node.js 18+ and was installed via npm (npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code). A native installer (curl | bash) was introduced later.

The curriculum needs to be verified against the CURRENT installation method. If the native installer is now the primary method, the content is correct. If npm is still the primary method, this entire mission teaches the wrong installation process. If BOTH methods exist, the curriculum should acknowledge both.

The quiz distractor 'Node.js 18 or higher' being marked as WRONG could be a factual error if the npm install path still exists as a supported method.
acceptance_criteria:
- Verify the current primary installation method for Claude Code
- If native installer is primary: content is fine, but consider mentioning npm as an alternative
- If npm is still primary/supported: this is a P0 factual error that needs immediate correction
- The quiz question and all references to installation must match the actual current process
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch2 merge

title: NoApiKey screen alignItems center squishes multi-line text blocks
description:
The NoApiKey screen (src/screens/NoApiKey.tsx lines 20-27) centers its inner content:

```tsx
<Box
  borderStyle="double"
  borderColor={COLORS.red}
  paddingX={2}
  paddingY={1}
  flexDirection="column"
  alignItems="center"
>
```

The \`alignItems="center"\` causes each child Box (containing multi-line text) to shrink-wrap to its content width and center-align. This is fine for the header \`[ SIGNAL INTERRUPTED ]\` and the single-line action prompt, but it center-aligns the setup instructions block (lines 41-57) which contains:
- "Setup Instructions:" (heading)
- "1. Get your API key at https://..." (long line)
- "2. Export it in your shell:" (medium line)
- "   export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-..." (code sample)

These instructional lines read better left-aligned. Centering multi-line instructional text with a numbered list is awkward — the numbers don't line up visually.

Compare with other screens: Logo centers everything (fine for a splash screen), but all instructional/content screens (Briefing, Credits, InfiniteMode) left-align their content within the border.

**Fix:** Remove \`alignItems="center"\` from the inner Box, or change it to only center the header and footer while letting the middle content blocks left-align. For example:
```tsx
<Box ... flexDirection="column">
  <Box justifyContent="center" marginBottom={1}>
    <Text color={COLORS.red} bold>[ SIGNAL INTERRUPTED ]</Text>
  </Box>
  {/* content blocks without centering */}
  <Box justifyContent="center" marginTop={1}>
    {/* action prompt */}
  </Box>
</Box>
```
This matches the pattern used in Credits, Debrief, and MissionMap.
close_reason: NoApiKey content alignment fixed. Commit afa3295.

title: Mission map '[C] Credits' keybinding only shown after full clearance — inconsistent with other post-game content
description: The mission map (MissionMap.tsx line 253) shows '[C] Credits' only when allComplete is true. This is intentionally gated behind completion. However, the key handler (line 66-68) also checks allComplete before processing 'c'. This is fine for credits specifically. But the inconsistency is that the keybinding text is shown at the bottom mixed with navigation hints, making it feel like an afterthought rather than a reward. Could be given more visual treatment — a gold-colored separate section or a pulsing indicator to celebrate full completion.

title: Debrief screen always shows 3/3 cover remaining regardless of actual damage taken
description: In app.tsx line 244, the Debrief screen receives a hardcoded `coverRemaining={3}` prop instead of the actual cover integrity value from the mission. This means even if the player took damage and lost cover blocks during a mission, the debrief always shows perfect cover. This is visible in every single mission completion and would embarrass in a demo.
notes: File: src/app.tsx line 244. The missionContext state already tracks stars and fxpEarned but does not track coverRemaining. Need to: (1) add coverRemaining to missionContext state, (2) pass actual cover from Mission onComplete callback, (3) thread it through to Debrief.
close_reason: Debrief cover fix merged. coverRemaining threaded from Mission through missionContext. Commit 49115f1.

title: Cover integrity indicator uses different visual representations on StatusBar vs Debrief
description:
The cover integrity (health) state is displayed in two places with completely different visual languages:

**StatusBar.tsx lines 37-39 (during mission):**
```tsx
const coverBlocks = Array.from({ length: 3 }, (_, i) =>
  i < coverIntegrity ? "\u25A0" : "\u25A1"
).join(" ");
```
Displays as filled/empty squares in brackets: \`[# # #]\`, \`[# # o]\`, \`[# o o]\`
Uses dynamic color: green when healthy, red when low/flashing.

**Debrief.tsx lines 101-105 (after mission):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  COVER INTEGRITY REMAINING: {coverRemaining}/3
</Text>
```
Displays as plain text number: "COVER INTEGRITY REMAINING: 3/3"
Uses warmWhite — no color coding.

The StatusBar uses a visual/graphical indicator (filled squares, dynamic color). The Debrief uses plain text with no visual treatment. The transition from a rich health-bar visual to "3/3" in plain text is a letdown, especially since the Debrief is a celebration/summary screen.

**Fix:** Use the same filled/empty square indicator on the Debrief that the StatusBar uses, with appropriate coloring:
```tsx
<Box marginBottom={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>COVER INTEGRITY: </Text>
  <Text color={coverRemaining === 3 ? COLORS.green : coverRemaining >= 2 ? COLORS.amber : COLORS.red} bold>
    {"\u25A0 ".repeat(coverRemaining)}{"\u25A1 ".repeat(3 - coverRemaining)}
  </Text>
</Box>
```
This carries the visual language from gameplay into the summary.
close_reason: Cover blocks in Debrief now match StatusBar. Commit 6f42bed.

Then Mission 10 (LEGEND BUILDER, line 1037) revisits /doctor with actual context and a command step testing it (line 1040-1053). The brief mention in Mission 05 steals the thunder of Mission 10's deeper treatment. Consider removing /doctor from Mission 05's slash command list and leaving it for Mission 10 to introduce properly.

title: Mission 12: --model flag command question tests knowledge never taught in the curriculum
description:
Mission 12 (CONTROL TOWER) asks: 'What flag lets you select a different AI model for cost optimization on simpler tasks?' Expected answer: '--model'. 

The preceding print step about cost optimization mentions 'Choose the right model tier for each task' but never introduces the --model flag. The player has no way to know the flag name.

This is the same pattern as Mission 10's /init question (cca-1nk) — testing before teaching. The fix is the same: either introduce the flag in a print step first, or convert to multiple choice.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a print step before this question that introduces the --model flag
- OR convert to multiple choice with plausible distractors like '--tier', '--engine', '--model', '--llm'
- The curriculum should teach before testing, always

title: Mission 04 command question 'Where do you place a CLAUDE.md' has vague expected answer
description:
Mission 04 (PHANTOM PROTOCOL) asks: 'Where do you place a CLAUDE.md for project-wide instructions?' Expected answer: 'project root'.

This is a natural-language answer, not a command or technical term. The accepted variants try to cover phrasings ('the project root', 'root of the project', 'repository root', 'repo root', './CLAUDE.md', etc.) but miss many reasonable answers:
- 'root directory'
- 'the root'
- 'base of the repo'
- 'top-level directory'
- 'project directory root'
- '.' (literally the project root)

This is a question about a location/concept, not a specific command. It should be multiple choice. The AI evaluator may catch some of these, but the local matcher will reject them, and the player sees 'COMPROMISED' before AI evaluation can help (or does the AI evaluator run first? The code shows AI evaluation runs on submit, but the response time matters for player experience).
acceptance_criteria:
- Convert to multiple choice: 'project root', '~/.claude/', '/etc/claude/', 'any subdirectory'
- OR significantly expand acceptedVariants to cover the natural-language answer space
- Conceptual location questions work better as multiple choice than free text
close_reason: Fixed in fix-hard-commands merge — converted to multiple-choice quizzes

title: Component tests: TypeWriter component
description:
Test file: `__tests__/components/TypeWriter.test.tsx`

Module under test: `src/components/TypeWriter.tsx`

## Test cases

### noAnimation mode
- Renders full text immediately when noAnimation=true
- Calls onComplete immediately when noAnimation=true
- Does not use intervals when noAnimation=true

### Animated mode
- Starts with empty rendered text (charIndex=0)
- Progressively reveals characters over time
- Calls onComplete after all characters are revealed
- Does NOT call onComplete before text is fully rendered
- Respects speed='fast' (TIMING.typewriterFast interval)
- Respects speed='normal' (TIMING.typewriterNormal interval)
- Respects speed='dramatic' (TIMING.typewriterDramatic interval)
- Default speed is 'normal'

### Text changes
- Resets charIndex when new text is shorter than current position
- Handles empty text string

### onComplete callback
- Only called once per text completion (not on re-renders)
- Not called if not provided (optional prop)

## Testing approach
- Use ink-testing-library to render the component
- Use vi.useFakeTimers() to control interval timing
- Advance timers with vi.advanceTimersByTime() and check rendered output
- Verify onComplete with vi.fn() mock
close_reason: Merged, tests passing

title: Returning player goes straight to mission map — no welcome back message or persistence check
description: When a returning player launches the game (app.tsx line 44: skipToMap), they jump directly to the mission map with no greeting, no 'welcome back', no summary of their progress. The checkPersistence function (achievements.ts line 94-104) does check if they have been away 7+ days and triggers the PERSISTENCE achievement, but for players returning after 1-6 days there is zero acknowledgment. A brief 'Welcome back, operative. Last seen X days ago. Current clearance: RECRUIT.' toast or status line would make the experience feel more personalized and alive. Even a simple 'Session resumed.' would be better than jumping to a cold mission map.

title: Bug: MissionMap displays fabricated FXP values instead of actual earned FXP
description: In src/screens/MissionMap.tsx line 299-301, the missionFxp() function calculates FXP as 'stars * 100 + 40' (yielding 140/240/340 for 1/2/3 stars). This has no relationship to actual gameplay FXP which is computed as 10 per correct answer + 5 per print step. For example, Mission 01 has 7 steps (4 print + 2 quiz + 1 command), so a perfect run earns 5*4 + 10*3 = 50 FXP, not 340. The MissionMap should either store per-mission FXP in SaveData and display the real value, or remove the per-mission FXP display entirely since only total FXP is currently persisted.
close_reason: Removed missionFxp() function and FXP display line from MissionRating. SaveData only persists total FXP, so per-mission display was always fabricated.

title: Onboarding text 'Learn Claude Code by doing.' feels abrupt and anticlimactic
description: The onboarding screen (src/screens/Onboarding.tsx) shows three lines: 1) 'You have been recruited by the Claude Code Terminal Training Division.' 2) 'Your mission: master the Claude Code CLI through field training.' 3) 'Learn Claude Code by doing.' The third line is the tagline/slogan, but shown in the typewriter sequence it feels abrupt and drops the spy theme. After two immersive spy-themed sentences, a marketing tagline breaks the fiction. Consider replacing it with something that maintains the spy conceit, e.g., 'Your field training begins now, recruit. Prove yourself worthy of full clearance.'

title: TypeWriter output has no color — inherits parent color inconsistently
description:
The TypeWriter component (src/components/TypeWriter.tsx line 69) renders its text inside a bare \`<Text>\` with no color prop:

```tsx
return <Text>{text.slice(0, charIndex)}</Text>;
```

Its color depends entirely on the parent wrapping it, leading to inconsistent text colors across screens:

- **Logo.tsx:55-61** — wrapped in \`<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>\`, so TypeWriter text is warmWhite
- **Onboarding.tsx:40-47** — no color wrapper at all, so TypeWriter text is the terminal default (likely white or gray depending on terminal theme)
- **Mission.tsx:135-141 (print steps)** — no wrapper, terminal default
- **Briefing.tsx:70-76** — wrapped in \`<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>\`

The Onboarding screen and Mission print steps render TypeWriter output in whatever the terminal's default foreground color is, which may or may not match \`COLORS.warmWhite\`. On terminals with light themes or non-standard colors, this could look noticeably different from screens that explicitly set warmWhite.

**Fix:** Either:
1. Add an optional \`color\` prop to TypeWriter and pass it through to the inner \`<Text>\` 
2. Default the TypeWriter's \`<Text>\` to \`color={COLORS.warmWhite}\` to match the established convention
3. Ensure all call sites wrap TypeWriter in a colored Text parent (the current approach in Logo and Briefing)

Option 2 is the smallest change and ensures consistency.
close_reason: TypeWriter color prop added. Commit 8d99efd.

title: README tech stack says 'Ink v6' and 'Zod 4' but package.json shows ink ^6.8.0 and zod ^4.3.6
description: The README tech stack table (line 167-174) says 'Ink v6' and 'Zod 4'. The package.json confirms ink ^6.8.0 and zod ^4.3.6, so the versions are accurate. However, the README also says 'React 19' while package.json shows 'react ^19.2.4'. These are all currently correct. Noting this for awareness that the README hardcodes version numbers that should stay in sync with package.json. Also the Ink link points to github.com/vadimdemedes/ink which is correct.

title: Mission 11: 'What should you NEVER put in CLAUDE.md' command question has too many valid answers
description:
Mission 11 (BURNED NOTICE) asks: 'What should you NEVER put in a CLAUDE.md or commit to version control?' Expected answer: 'API keys'. The accepted variants include 'API keys', 'secrets', 'passwords', 'credentials', 'sensitive data', 'tokens', etc.

While the variant list is generous, this is a classic example of a question better suited to multiple choice. The problem space is so broad that a learner might reasonably type:
- 'private keys'
- 'access tokens' 
- 'database passwords'
- 'connection strings'
- 'SSH keys'
- 'secret keys' (this one IS accepted but the line is arbitrary)

Any of these are correct but might not match the variant list or pass AI evaluation. The question is testing recall of a general security principle, not a specific Claude Code concept — multiple choice would be more appropriate and less frustrating.
acceptance_criteria:
- Convert to a quiz (multiple-choice) step with options like: 'API keys and secrets', 'Coding conventions', 'Architecture decisions', 'Test commands'
- OR significantly expand acceptedVariants to cover the full range of reasonable answers
- The question should test understanding, not the ability to guess which synonym the game expects
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch2 merge

title: Achievement says 'Complete all 8 missions' but game has 12 missions
description: The FULL_CLEARANCE achievement in src/lib/achievements.ts (line 44) says 'Complete all 8 missions' but the game has 12 missions. This is a factual error visible to players who check their achievements. The description should read 'Complete all 12 missions' to match the actual curriculum in src/data/curriculum.ts.
close_reason: Duplicate of cca-i789

title: Hardcoded 40-character separator lines don't adapt to terminal width
description: Multiple screens render hardcoded 40-character separator lines (─ repeated 40 times) that look odd on terminals wider or narrower than expected: Credits.tsx lines 75 and 131, MissionMap.tsx line 132, InfiniteMode.tsx lines 330 and 378. These should be computed from available width or use Ink's Box borderStyle for visual separation instead of raw character strings.
notes: Files: src/screens/Credits.tsx lines 75, 131. src/screens/MissionMap.tsx line 132. src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx lines 330, 378. Consider extracting a <Separator /> component that uses useTerminalSize or accepts a width prop, or use Ink's built-in border rendering.
close_reason: Separator lines now adapt to terminal width. Commit 9c41014.

title: Mission 07: --dangerously-skip-permissions is 32 characters — unreasonable as free-text input
description:
Mission 07 (IRON CURTAIN) asks: 'What CLI flag disables all permission checks for Claude Code?' Expected answer: '--dangerously-skip-permissions'.

This is a 32-character flag name. While it is intentionally long and memorable by design (the curriculum even notes this), expecting a player to type it perfectly from memory is unreasonable. The accepted variants include 'dangerously-skip-permissions' and 'dangerously skip permissions' but miss common partial recalls:
- '--skip-permissions' (reasonable abbreviation)
- '--no-permissions' 
- '--dangerous' (only remembering the first word)

This is one of the longest expected answers in the entire curriculum. At a minimum it should be multiple choice. The irony is that the flag's deliberately alarming name makes it MORE recognizable (perfect for multiple choice) but HARDER to type correctly (terrible for free text).
acceptance_criteria:
- Convert to multiple choice with options like '--dangerously-skip-permissions', '--skip-permissions', '--no-approve', '--auto-accept'
- This tests recognition of the deliberately scary flag name, which is the actual learning objective
- Free-text typing of a 32-character hyphenated string tests typing, not understanding
close_reason: Fixed in fix-hard-commands merge — converted to multiple-choice quizzes

title: Mission 01 quiz 'Which of these can Claude Code do directly' has weak distractors
description:
Mission 01 (FIRST CONTACT) quiz step asks: 'Which of these can Claude Code do directly?' Options:
A) Deploy to production servers
B) Read files and edit code in your project  
C) Replace your entire development team
D) Write code only in JavaScript

Options C and D are jokes/obvious wrong answers. C is absurd ('replace your entire team') and D includes 'only' which is a classic test-taking giveaway. A savvy player can eliminate C and D without any knowledge of Claude Code, leaving a 50/50 between A and B.

Option A ('Deploy to production servers') is the only plausible distractor, but even it feels like a stretch since the preceding text focuses on local file operations.

This is the player's SECOND quiz question ever. Better distractors here would build confidence that the game is testing real knowledge, not asking trick-free gimmes.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replace options C and D with plausible but incorrect capabilities:
  - 'Automatically fix all bugs in your code without review' 
  - 'Access and modify files on remote servers'
  - 'Run code in a sandboxed browser environment'
  - 'Connect to external databases directly'
- All four options should require the player to think, not just eliminate absurdities
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch5 — wording, distractors, slash command quizzes

title: Test infrastructure: vitest config, ink-testing-library, mock utilities
description:
Set up the foundational test infrastructure required by all other test tasks.

## Deliverables

### 1. vitest.config.ts
- Create explicit vitest config at project root
- Configure ESM support (type: module already in package.json)
- Set test directory to include both `__tests__/` and any colocated `.test.ts` files
- Configure globals: false (explicit imports preferred)
- Set reasonable timeouts (10s default, 30s for integration)
- Add coverage configuration (v8 provider, branches + functions + lines thresholds at 80%)
- Configure environment: 'node' for unit tests

### 2. Install dev dependencies
- `ink-testing-library` (for rendering Ink components in tests)
- `@vitest/coverage-v8` (for coverage reports)
- Verify `vitest` v4 compatibility with all additions

### 3. Test helpers and mocks (`__tests__/helpers/`)
Create reusable test utilities:

**`__tests__/helpers/mock-progress.ts`**
- Factory function to create SaveData objects with sensible defaults and overrides
- `createSaveData(overrides?: Partial<SaveData>): SaveData`
- `createCompletedSaveData()` -- all missions complete, achievements populated

**`__tests__/helpers/mock-missions.ts`**
- Minimal mission fixtures for tests that need Mission objects without importing the real curriculum
- `createMission(overrides?)` factory
- `createQuizStep(overrides?)`, `createCommandStep(overrides?)`, `createPrintStep(overrides?)`, `createAIStep(overrides?)` factories

**`__tests__/helpers/mock-ai.ts`**
- Vitest mock setup for `@anthropic-ai/sdk`
- Helper to simulate streaming responses from the Anthropic client
- Helper to simulate API errors (401, 429, 529, network errors)

**`__tests__/helpers/render-ink.ts`**
- Wrapper around ink-testing-library's render that provides common test setup
- Helper to simulate key presses (enter, escape, arrow keys, character input)
- Type-safe wrapper for common assertion patterns on Ink output

### 4. Test scripts in package.json
- `test` (already exists: `vitest run`)
- `test:watch` -> `vitest`
- `test:coverage` -> `vitest run --coverage`
- `test:unit` -> `vitest run --exclude='**/*.integration.test.*' --exclude='**/*.e2e.test.*'`
- `test:integration` -> `vitest run --include='**/*.integration.test.*'`

### Acceptance criteria
- `npm run test` continues to pass with all existing tests
- `npm run test:coverage` generates a coverage report
- ink-testing-library renders a simple `<Text>hello</Text>` component successfully in a test
- All mock factories produce valid typed objects
close_reason: Merged. 174 tests passing. ink-testing-library + mock helpers ready.

title: Expand acceptedVariants across all 20 command steps

title: Mission 05 lists 6 slash commands but only tests 2 — missed learning opportunity
description:
Mission 05 (SIGNAL CHAIN) has a print step that lists 6 slash commands: /help, /cost, /compact, /clear, /config, /doctor. But the mission only tests /compact (quiz), /cost (command), and /clear (command). /help, /config, and /doctor are listed but never tested.

/doctor appears later in Mission 10 as a quiz, but /help and /config are never tested anywhere in the entire 12-mission curriculum. Players are exposed to them in a list but never practice recalling them.

Learning science: information that is only read (not tested) has much lower retention. If these commands are important enough to list, they are important enough to quiz.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a quiz question about /config (what it does, not the name) in Mission 05 or Mission 07 (permissions/settings)
- /help can be mentioned as a meta-hint in the game UI rather than a curriculum item
- Consider removing commands from the list that are not tested — a shorter list that is fully tested is better than a longer list that is partially tested
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch5 — wording, distractors, slash command quizzes

title: NoApiKey screen says 'AI-powered missions require an API key' — misleading, missions work without it
description: The NoApiKey screen (src/screens/NoApiKey.tsx line 43-44) says 'AI-powered missions require an API key to function.' This is misleading. Missions work fine without an API key — quizzes use multiple choice and command steps fall back to local matching + self-assessment. Only the Handler overlay and Infinite Mode truly require the API key. The text should accurately say something like 'Some features (AI handler, free-text evaluation, Infinite Mode) require an API key. The core curriculum works without one.' This matches what the README says: 'The core curriculum works without any API key.'
close_reason: Duplicate of cca-xydf

title: Command step 'Verifying...' shows during AI evaluation but gives no timeout or fallback
description:
When a player submits a command answer, the CommandStep component enters the 'pausing' phase and displays 'Verifying...' while the AI evaluator runs. The evaluateAnswer function makes an API call to the Haiku model.

If the API call is slow (network issues, rate limiting, cold start), the player stares at 'Verifying...' indefinitely with:
- No timeout
- No loading animation (just static gray text)
- No way to cancel or skip
- No indication that an API call is happening or how long it might take

Looking at the code, there is a guard 'if (!result) return;' after the evaluator call, but no timeout wrapping the await. If evaluateAnswer hangs or takes 10+ seconds, the player is stuck.

The QuizStep (multiple choice) does NOT have this problem because it compares locally with no API call. The asymmetry in response time between quiz and command steps is jarring.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a timeout (e.g., 5 seconds) to the AI evaluation call
- If timeout is reached: fall back to local matching only and show the result
- Add a subtle animation to 'Verifying...' (dots cycling, or a spinner) to show the game is not frozen
- Consider showing 'Verifying with headquarters...' to maintain the spy theme while indicating network activity
close_reason: Fixed — eval timeout with fallback + step progress indicator

title: Mission map pulsing animation for 'IN PROGRESS' runs continuously — potential CPU/render cost
description: MissionMap.tsx (line 48-51) runs a setInterval every 500ms to toggle pulseBright, which causes the 'IN PROGRESS' text and current mission indicator to pulse. This interval runs continuously whenever the mission map is mounted, even when the player is not looking at it (e.g., in a briefing or mission with the map still mounted in background). The re-render triggered by this state change every 500ms affects the entire mission map component. Should cleanup or pause when not visible. Minor performance concern.

title: Inconsistent border style between main screens and overlays
description:
The app uses two border styles — `double` for main screens and `single` for overlays — but this convention is broken in one place and lacks clarity in another:

**The inconsistency:**
- Main screens use `borderStyle="double"`: Logo, Briefing, MissionMap, Debrief, InfiniteMode, Credits, NoApiKey, Achievement
- Overlays use `borderStyle="single"`: Handler, HelpOverlay
- **Quit confirmation dialog** (src/app.tsx line 294) uses `borderStyle="single"` — this is a modal dialog like the overlays, so "single" is defensible, but it is styled with `borderColor={COLORS.amber}` which is unique among all bordered containers

**The border color mismatch on quit dialog:**
The quit confirmation at src/app.tsx:294-303 uses:
- `borderStyle="single"` + `borderColor={COLORS.amber}`

Every other `borderStyle="single"` container uses `borderColor={COLORS.cyan}` (Handler at line 151, HelpOverlay at line 94). The amber border makes the quit dialog look like it belongs to a different app.

**Fix:** Change the quit confirmation dialog's `borderColor` to `COLORS.cyan` to match the other single-bordered overlays, or change its `borderStyle` to `"double"` to match modal/alert dialogs (like NoApiKey which uses double+red for warnings). The amber text inside is enough to signal the warning nature.
close_reason: Quit dialog border standardized. Commit 14e2614.

title: Phase 3: Content Expansion

title: InfiniteMode FXP stale state in saveInfiniteResult
description: InfiniteMode.tsx:168 calls `saveInfiniteResult(newCorrect, newTotal, fxpEarned + fxp)` but `fxpEarned` is the pre-update state value (the `setFxpEarned` on line 163 hasn't flushed yet). On the final question, saved FXP is short by the current question's value. Fix: compute `const totalFxp = fxpEarned + fxp` and use it for both `setFxpEarned` and `saveInfiniteResult`.

title: Replace free-text install command question with multiple choice
description: Mission 2 step asks 'What command installs Claude Code on macOS or Linux?' as a free-text command input expecting 'curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash'. Nobody will type that from memory. Convert to a multiple choice quiz question instead.

title: Bug: AI steps render as passive TypeWriter text instead of interactive prompts
description: In src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 211-218, the 'ai' step type renders as a plain TypeWriter that displays step.prompt as read-only text and calls handlePrintComplete (+5 FXP). The player simply reads the prompt and presses Enter to continue, identical to a print step. However, AIStep is designed to be interactive -- missions 10, 11, and 12 each have AI prompts like 'Write a short example CLAUDE.md section' and 'Describe one creative way a team could use Claude Code' that expect the player to compose a response. Without an input mechanism, these steps have zero pedagogical value. The AI step should render a text input, send the player's response to the AI evaluator for feedback (when hasApiKey is true), or at minimum show the prompt and let the player type a response. The types.ts AIStep type only has a 'prompt' field with no onAnswer callback, confirming this feature was never fully implemented.
close_reason: Closed

title: CommandStep evalFailed logic auto-passes wrong answers
description: CommandStep.tsx:110 has `const effectiveCorrect = isCorrect || !!failed`. When eval fails and local match says wrong (isCorrect=false, failed=true), the answer is treated as correct — player gets FXP and no cover damage for a wrong answer. This rewards incorrect answers during API outages. Consider: advance without scoring (no FXP gain, no cover penalty) instead of auto-passing.
close_reason: Fixed and merged to main. evalFailed now advances without scoring.

title: Debrief section labels lack consistent visual hierarchy with rest of app
description:
The Debrief screen (src/screens/Debrief.tsx) displays its sections with inconsistent label styling compared to both itself and the rest of the app:

**Debrief internal inconsistency:**
- Star rating: no label, just centered gold stars (line 87-90)
- FXP: \`FXP EARNED:\` in green bold (line 95-96) — ALL CAPS label + value
- Cover: \`COVER INTEGRITY REMAINING:\` in warmWhite non-bold (line 102-103) — ALL CAPS but not bold
- Objectives: \`OBJECTIVES:\` in warmWhite bold (line 109) — ALL CAPS and bold

The same screen uses three different label treatments:
1. Green + bold (FXP)
2. WarmWhite + no bold (Cover)
3. WarmWhite + bold (Objectives)

**Comparison with other screens:**
- Briefing.tsx uses aligned labels: \`MISSION:\`, \`CLEARANCE:\`, \`HANDLER:\` all in warmWhite
- MissionMap.tsx uses cyan for labels: \`CLEARANCE:\`, \`FXP:\`
- InfiniteMode summary uses warmWhite labels with amber bold values

**Fix:** Standardize the Debrief labels to all use the same treatment. Recommended: warmWhite for labels, amber/gold bold for values — matching the StatusBar pattern:

```
FXP EARNED:                75
COVER INTEGRITY:           [# # o]
OBJECTIVES:
  (checkmarks)
```

All labels in \`COLORS.warmWhite\`, all value content in appropriate semantic colors (amber for FXP, green/red for cover, green for completed objectives).
close_reason: Debrief sections use cyan+bold headers. Commit d7bea14.

title: Mission 02 command step asks user to recall a 47-character curl URL from memory
description: Mission 02 (DEAD DROP) has a command-type question asking: 'What command installs Claude Code on macOS or Linux?' The expected answer is 'curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash' — a 47-character string with flags, a URL, and a pipe. No reasonable learner will type this from memory, especially on their second mission. This is already tracked in cca-5x2 for this specific question, but the underlying pattern applies to other command steps too.
design: Already partially tracked in cca-5x2. Consider whether this issue is a duplicate or should be closed in favor of that one. If kept, scope it to ensuring the fix in cca-5x2 is comprehensive.
acceptance_criteria:
- This question should be converted to a multiple-choice quiz step with plausible distractor install commands
- OR provide the URL in the question text and only ask the user to type the command structure
close_reason: Duplicate of existing issue cca-5x2 which already tracks replacing the install command question with multiple choice.

title: Mission map vertical connector shows ':' for incomplete and '|' for complete — meaning unclear
description: MissionMap.tsx (line 204-211) renders a vertical connector between missions: '|' for completed missions and ':' for incomplete. This visual distinction is subtle and its meaning is not explained anywhere. The ':' (dotted) vs '|' (solid) metaphor mimics a connected/disconnected path, which is thematic. But without explanation, it looks like a rendering artifact. Consider using a more obvious visual: solid line for connected/completed, dimmed '...' for locked path, or removing the distinction entirely since the [x]/[>]/[ ] indicators already communicate status.

title: Mission map Deep Cover Operations shows ?? emoji — may not render on all terminals
description: MissionMap.tsx line 226 shows '?? DEEP COVER OPERATIONS' using literal question mark characters. While these render fine on all terminals, the '??' prefix looks like placeholder text or a rendering error rather than an intentional design choice. The locked version (line 233) also uses '?? ' followed by block characters. The question marks are presumably meant to suggest mystery/unknown, but they read as 'unknown/broken' in a terminal context. Consider using a more intentional character like a diamond, star, or infinity symbol, or simply removing the prefix.

title: QuizStep has marginLeft={1} baseline shift that other step types lack
description:
In src/components/QuizStep.tsx line 110, the root container has a baseline left margin:

```tsx
<Box
  flexDirection="column"
  gap={1}
  marginLeft={SHAKE_BASE_MARGIN + shakeOffset}
>
```

Where \`SHAKE_BASE_MARGIN = 1\` (line 19). This means quiz questions are always indented 1 column to the right compared to other step types. The margin exists so the shake animation on wrong answers can shift left (offset -1) without going negative.

CommandStep (src/components/CommandStep.tsx line 119) has no marginLeft:
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" gap={1}>
```

Print steps (TypeWriter) also have no margin.

This means during a mission, when the user moves from a print step to a quiz step to a command step, the content horizontally shifts left and right. The question text in a quiz is 1 column further right than a command question, even though both use the same color and style (\`color={COLORS.cyan} bold\`).

**Fix:** Either:
1. Add \`marginLeft={1}\` to CommandStep and wrap print steps similarly, so all step content is uniformly indented
2. Change the shake animation to use a different approach that doesn't require a baseline offset (e.g., pad the question text with spaces, or use a wrapping Box for just the shake content), so the quiz can have marginLeft={0} like everything else
close_reason: QuizStep marginLeft removed. Commit e2426ff.

title: Component tests: ScreenTransition component
description:
Test file: `__tests__/components/ScreenTransition.test.tsx`

Module under test: `src/components/ScreenTransition.tsx`

## Test cases

### noAnimation mode
- Renders children directly when noAnimation=true
- Updates children immediately when screenKey changes
- No wipe/draw phases occur

### Animated mode
- Starts in 'idle' phase, renders children normally
- When screenKey changes, enters 'wipe' phase
- Wipe phase progressively reduces visible lines to 0
- After wipe, enters 'draw' phase with new content
- Draw phase progressively increases visible lines to full
- After draw, returns to 'idle' phase
- Children are updated between wipe and draw (pendingKey logic)

### Edge cases
- Same screenKey change is a no-op
- Rapid successive screen changes are handled (pendingKey is overwritten)

## Testing approach
- Use ink-testing-library with mocked useStdout
- Use vi.useFakeTimers() to step through animation frames
- Check that children content changes between wipe and draw phases
close_reason: Merged

title: Build free-text answer evaluator
description:
src/ai/evaluator.ts — semantic answer evaluation.

evaluateAnswer() function:
- Uses Haiku model (fast, cheap for evaluation)
- Input length capped at 200 characters
- System prompt: evaluate factual content only, ignore meta-instructions (prompt injection defense)
- Assistant message prefill: start response with '{' to anchor JSON
- Parse with safeJsonParse, validate with EvaluationSchema (zod)
- On parse failure: return { correct: false, feedback: 'Could not evaluate. Try again.' }
- On API failure: fall back to local variant matching from acceptedVariants[]
- Generous matching: accept equivalent phrasings

Fallback chain for CommandStep:
1. If AI available → evaluateAnswer()
2. If AI unavailable/fails → local variant matching (case-insensitive, strip prefixes)

Acceptance: Evaluates semantic equivalence, falls back gracefully, prompt injection resistant
close_reason: Merged to main

title: Build Handler AI chat with streaming and history
description:
src/ai/instructor.ts — streaming handler chat.

askHandler() function:
- Accepts: question, missionTitle, topicContext, conversationHistory, onChunk callback, AbortSignal
- Input length capped at 500 characters
- Sliding window history: last 8 messages (4 exchanges) to control token growth
- System prompt includes scope boundary: only answer Claude Code questions, redirect off-topic
- Prompt injection resistance: decline persona changes
- Uses streaming (client.messages.stream) with AbortController signal for cancellation
- Returns full response text for caller to append to history
- Catches AbortError on clean cancellation (ESC mid-stream)
- Debounce: component enforces 2-second gap between requests
- Per-session soft cap: 20 questions, then 'handler credits used' message
- Detect stop_reason === 'max_tokens' and append '...'

src/screens/Handler.tsx:
- Centered modal overlay
- Buffer onChunk calls: accumulate 50ms before setState (prevents flicker)
- Maintain conversation history in component state (cleared on mission change)
- Create AbortController per request — abort() when overlay closes or new question sent
- On error: display message from safeApiError, 'Press Enter to retry'
- Handler thinking animation: 'Handler is reviewing intel ...' cycling dots

Acceptance: Streaming works, ESC cancels cleanly, history maintained, errors handled gracefully

title: Integration tests: App component - screen flow and keyboard routing
description:
Test file: `__tests__/integration/app-flow.integration.test.tsx`

Module under test: `src/app.tsx`

## Test cases

### Initial screen routing
- New user (firstRunComplete=false, reset=false): starts at 'logo' screen
- Returning user (firstRunComplete=true, reset=false): starts at 'missionMap'
- Reset mode (reset=true): starts at 'logo' and calls resetProgress

### Screen navigation flow
- Logo -> (ENTER) -> Onboarding (first-time user)
- Logo -> (ENTER) -> MissionMap (returning user)
- Onboarding -> (complete) -> MissionMap
- MissionMap -> (select mission) -> Briefing
- Briefing -> (accept) -> Mission
- Mission -> (complete) -> Debrief
- Debrief -> (continue) -> MissionMap
- MissionMap -> (select infinite) -> InfiniteMode
- InfiniteMode -> (back) -> MissionMap
- MissionMap -> (credits) -> Credits
- Credits -> (close) -> MissionMap

### Keyboard routing
- ESC closes overlay when overlay is open
- ESC navigates to missionMap from mission screen
- ESC navigates to missionMap from briefing screen
- '?' opens handler overlay during mission (when hasApiKey=true)
- '?' does nothing when hasApiKey=false
- 'h' opens help overlay (when not in active input)
- 'q' on missionMap prompts quit confirmation
- 'y'/'Y' on quit confirmation exits app
- Any other key on quit confirmation dismisses it

### Overlay management
- Handler overlay shows when state.overlay.handler is true
- Help overlay shows when state.overlay.help is true
- Overlays close when navigating to new screen
- Only one overlay type should be primary at a time (though both can be true)

### Achievement system integration
- checkPersistence called on mount and result enqueued
- updateLastPlayed called on mount
- Achievement notification shows when queue is non-empty
- Achievement dismisses and next one shows from queue
- checkMissionComplete called after mission completion
- checkHandlerOpen called after handler opens

### Mission completion flow
- saveMissionComplete called with correct mission ID, stars, and fxp
- Duration is tracked from mission start to completion
- Stars and fxp passed to Debrief via missionContext

## Mocking strategy
- Mock ALL store/progress.js functions
- Mock ALL lib/achievements.js functions
- Mock evaluateAnswer for CommandStep (avoid real AI calls)
- Use ink-testing-library for rendering
- Simulate keyboard sequences to drive navigation
- This is a complex integration test -- may need to break into multiple test files

## Important notes
- App component has many dependencies -- ensure mocks are comprehensive
- Focus on the happy path first, then edge cases
- The useInput hook's isActive flag means keyboard routing changes with screen state
close_reason: App flow integration tests merged. 16 tests, commit 5469e64.

title: Mission 03: 'six primary tools' list may be incomplete or inaccurate
description:
Mission 03 (BRASS COMPASS) teaches: 'Claude Code's core toolset includes six primary tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep.'

This list may be incomplete. Claude Code also has:
- Agent/TodoWrite (subagent spawning, mentioned in Mission 08)
- WebSearch or WebFetch (web capabilities, if available)
- Notebook tools (for Jupyter notebooks)

The 'six tools' framing creates a false sense of completeness. If a player later encounters Claude Code using a tool not on this list, they may be confused.

Additionally, the list omits the Agent tool which the curriculum itself discusses in Mission 08. The curriculum contradicts itself: Mission 03 says 'six primary tools' but Mission 08 introduces the Agent tool as if it is also a core capability.

The fix is simple: say 'core file and search tools' instead of 'six primary tools', or acknowledge that there are additional tools beyond these six.
acceptance_criteria:
- Either expand the list to include all commonly-used tools, OR
- Change the framing from 'six primary tools' to 'core file and search tools include...' to avoid implying completeness
- Acknowledge the Agent tool exists (can be a brief mention even if full coverage is in Mission 08)
- Verify the tool list against the current Claude Code tool set
close_reason: Fixed — tool count, /init description, permissions format updated

title: Security check module only runs at import time — results are stale if env changes
description: security-check.ts runs all checks (hardcoded key detection, .env in dist, etc.) at module import time and caches the results. If the module is imported once during app startup, the security checks reflect the state at startup. This is fine for the current use case but means any dynamic security check (e.g. checking if a key was accidentally logged) would need a separate mechanism. Currently not a bug, but the design is brittle if the module is ever used for runtime checks.
notes: File: src/lib/security-check.ts. Low priority — the current usage is correct (one-time startup check). Flag this if runtime security checks are ever needed.
close_reason: Security check lazy init. Commit 21dd62a.

title: Mission 12 model flag example uses outdated model ID 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001'
description: Mission 12 (curriculum.ts line 1282) teaches the --model flag with the example: 'claude --model claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 "format this file"'. This hardcodes a specific model ID with a date-based version string. When newer model versions are released, this example will become outdated and potentially confusing. Instead of teaching a specific versioned model ID, the example should use a more general reference or note that model names change over time: 'claude --model claude-haiku-4-5 "format this file"' (without the date suffix) or teach the concept more abstractly.
close_reason: Replaced claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 with 'haiku' alias and added guidance on using short aliases in curriculum.ts Mission 12

Large ASCII block-letter CLAUDE logo that types itself onto screen line-by-line.

Content:
- Block letter CLAUDE in double-border box

title: Mission 08: --output-format command question is unnecessarily hard as free-text
description:
Mission 08 (DEEP COVER) asks: 'What flag controls the output format in headless mode?' Expected answer: '--output-format'.

This is a 15-character flag with a hyphen in the middle. While the preceding print step does mention '--output-format', expecting the user to type it exactly from memory is more a test of short-term recall than understanding. Compare with simpler command answers like 'claude', '/cost', '/clear', 'Edit', 'Grep' — this one is significantly harder to type correctly.

Common typos/variants a user might try:
- '--output-format json' (includes the value — IS in acceptedVariants)
- '--format' (reasonable shortening — NOT accepted)
- 'output-format' (without dashes — IS accepted)
- '--outputformat' (no hyphen — NOT accepted)
- '--output_format' (underscore — NOT accepted)

This should be multiple choice.
acceptance_criteria:
- Convert to a multiple-choice quiz with options like '--output-format', '--format', '--json', '--response-type'
- OR add more accepted variants: '--format', '--outputformat', '--output_format'
- Flags with complex names are better tested via recognition (multiple choice) than recall (free text)
close_reason: Fixed in fix-hard-commands merge — converted to multiple-choice quizzes

title: Instructor session question count and debounce timer never reset between missions
description: In instructor.ts, the module-level `sessionQuestionCount` (capped at 20 per session) and `lastRequestTime` (2-second debounce) persist across the entire app lifetime. If a user opens the handler in mission 1, asks 15 questions, then plays mission 5 and opens the handler again, they only have 5 questions remaining in their session cap. The debounce timer also carries over — if they asked a question at the end of one mission, the 2-second cooldown applies at the start of the next handler session. This is confusing because the handler overlay is presented as a fresh conversation each time.
notes: File: src/ai/instructor.ts. The session cap (MAX_SESSION_QUESTIONS=20) and lastRequestTime should reset when the handler overlay opens for a new mission. Options: (1) Export a resetSession() function called by the Handler component on mount, (2) Accept the session counter as a parameter, (3) Use a class instance instead of module state so each Handler mount gets a fresh instance.
close_reason: Instructor state resets. Commit 2336bd7.

title: No way to skip typewriter animation — stuck watching text on replay
description:
When replaying a mission (after failing or for practice), the player must sit through every typewriter animation again with no way to skip. The TypeWriter component has no skip/fast-forward mechanism — it animates character by character at the configured speed until completion, then transitions to the 'waitEnter' phase.

For a first-time player, the animation is atmospheric. For a replaying player who already read the text, it is torturous. This is especially bad when combined with the 3-strikes 'cover blown' mechanic — if you fail a mission near the end, you restart and must re-watch ALL the print steps from the beginning.

A mission with 6 print steps averaging 200 characters at normal speed (20ms/char) = ~24 seconds of forced re-watching. At dramatic speed, it is even worse.
acceptance_criteria:
- Pressing any key (Enter, Space, or any character) during a typewriter animation should instantly complete it (show full text)
- After skipping, the 'press Enter to continue' prompt should appear immediately
- The noAnimation config flag already exists but is all-or-nothing; this is about per-step user-initiated skipping
- Should work on both print steps and the briefing screen typewriter
notes: Use the common video game convention: pressing Enter (or any key) during a typewriter animation instantly completes the text, showing it all at once. This is standard UX in RPGs/visual novels and players will instinctively try it. Implementation: detect keypress during TypeWriter animation, set charIndex to text.length immediately.
close_reason: Fixed: TypeWriter skip on keypress, noAnimation for seen steps on cover-blown restart, dramatic→normal for long paragraphs

title: Bug: Duplicate '/clear' command question in missions 5 and 6
description: In src/data/curriculum.ts, Mission 05 (SIGNAL CHAIN, line 402) asks 'What slash command clears your conversation history and starts fresh?' with expectedAnswer '/clear', and Mission 06 (GLASS HIGHWAY, line 540) asks 'What slash command clears your conversation entirely to start fresh?' also with expectedAnswer '/clear'. The player encounters the same command recall question twice across consecutive missions, reducing educational value and feeling repetitive. One of these should be replaced with a different question or the '/clear' content should be consolidated into one mission.

title: Handler Error state says 'Press Enter to retry' but actually retries the last question, not user's input
description: The Handler error state (src/screens/Handler.tsx line 193-196) shows the error message and 'Press Enter to retry'. The retry handler (line 122-128) retries lastQuestionRef.current, not whatever the user typed in the input field. This means if the user typed a new question after the error, pressing Enter retries the old question while the new input is cleared. The behavior is technically correct (retry means retry) but the UX is confusing because the input field still has focus and the user may have started typing a new question. Should either disable the input during error state or more clearly indicate 'Press Enter to retry your last question'.

title: InfiniteMode generates 5 questions but EST_COST_PER_BATCH uses Sonnet — cost may be understated
description: InfiniteMode (src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx line 49) shows EST_COST_PER_BATCH = 0.02. The quiz generator (src/ai/quiz-gen.ts) uses MODELS.GENERATOR which is 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514'. Sonnet is significantly more expensive than Haiku. At Sonnet pricing, 5 questions with ~1500 max tokens output could cost more than $0.02. The estimate should be verified against current Anthropic pricing for Sonnet 4 and updated if needed. This is an accuracy concern since the player is being told what to expect financially.

description:
src/lib/terminal.ts — terminal size utilities.

Requirements:
- useStdout() to get terminal dimensions

title: Mission screen lacks outer padding/alignment, unlike all other main screens
description:
The Mission screen (src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 167-214) uses a different layout structure than every other main screen:

**Every other screen wraps content in:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" alignItems="center" justifyContent="center" padding={1}>
  <Box borderStyle="double" borderColor={COLORS.cyan} paddingX={2} paddingY={1}>
    {/* content */}
  </Box>
</Box>
```

**Mission screen uses:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" width="100%" height="100%">
  <StatusBar ... />
  <Box flexDirection="column" flexGrow={1} paddingX={1} paddingY={1}>
    {/* content */}
  </Box>
  <BottomBar ... />
</Box>
```

This is intentionally full-width (it needs StatusBar and BottomBar), but the inner content area uses \`paddingX={1}\` while other screens use \`paddingX={2}\` for their content inside the border. Since Mission has no border, the content sits 1 column from the edge, whereas bordered screens have their content 2 columns from the border (which itself is 1 column from the edge via the outer \`padding={1}\`) = 4 columns from the edge total.

This means quiz questions and command prompts during a mission sit much closer to the terminal edge than the same kind of content would appear on other screens, creating an inconsistent reading position.

**Fix:** Change the Mission content area from \`paddingX={1}\` to \`paddingX={2}\` to give the same horizontal breathing room the rest of the app provides. This is a one-line change at src/screens/Mission.tsx line 179.
close_reason: Mission paddingX fixed to 2. Commit b53946c.

title: BottomBar action hints duplicate in-content prompts during waitEnter phase
description:
During the mission "waitEnter" phase (after a print step), the user sees TWO "[ENTER] continue" prompts simultaneously:

1. **In the content area** (src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 198-204):
```tsx
{phase === "waitEnter" && (
  <Box marginTop={1}>
    <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
      [ENTER] continue
    </Text>
  </Box>
)}
```

2. **In the BottomBar** (src/components/BottomBar.tsx line 11):
```tsx
continue: "[ENTER] continue",
```

Both display "[ENTER] continue" — one in the content area (amber, bold) and one in the bottom bar (also amber). The user sees the same action hint twice on screen, which looks redundant and clutters the interface.

Similarly, during "coverBlown" phase, Mission.tsx:191 shows \`[ENTER] Restart mission\` in the content area while BottomBar shows \`[ENTER] continue\` at the bottom — which is actually contradictory (it says "continue" but the action is "restart").

**Fix:** Either:
1. Remove the inline "[ENTER] continue" from the content area during waitEnter, relying solely on the BottomBar for keybinding hints (this is the BottomBar's entire purpose)
2. Or suppress the BottomBar's "continue" action during waitEnter/coverBlown since the content area already has a more contextual prompt

Option 1 is cleaner — the BottomBar should be the single source of truth for available actions.
close_reason: Duplicate prompts removed from content area. Commit 6f42bed.

title: Inconsistent separator style: InfiniteMode summary uses em-dash header while others use full-width lines
description:
The app has two different separator/divider styles that are used inconsistently:

**Style 1 — Full-width 40-char line (used in most places):**
- MissionMap.tsx:132: \`"────────────────────────────────────────"\`
- Credits.tsx:75,131: \`"────────────────────────────────────────"\`
- InfiniteMode.tsx:330,378: \`"────────────────────────────────────────"\`

All of these use \`color={COLORS.gray}\` and serve as horizontal dividers.

**Style 2 — Em-dash inline header (used once):**
- InfiniteMode.tsx:397: \`"── ASSESSMENT COMPLETE ──"\`

This is a different visual convention — a centered label flanked by short em-dash segments. It appears only once and looks like a section header rather than a divider. No other screen uses this pattern.

(Note: cca-5b5 already covers the fact that the 40-char lines are hardcoded widths. This issue is about the stylistic inconsistency, not the width problem.)

**Fix:** Change the InfiniteMode summary header to use the standard bracketed header pattern: \`[ ASSESSMENT COMPLETE ]\` with \`color={COLORS.cyan} bold\`, consistent with every other screen's header (e.g., \`[ MISSION DEBRIEF ]\`, \`[ MISSION SELECT ]\`, etc.). This eliminates the one-off em-dash header style entirely.
close_reason: InfiniteMode summary uses bracketed header. Commit d7bea14.
