title: Module-level mutable state in instructor.ts and easter-eggs.ts is not resettable for testing
description: Two modules use module-level mutable variables that persist across test runs and can't be reset without module re-loading: (1) instructor.ts has `lastRequestTime` and `sessionQuestionCount` at module scope — these track debounce timing and session caps but leak between tests. (2) easter-eggs.ts has `lastMessageIndex` at module scope — controls loading message rotation. Neither module exports a reset function. Tests currently work around this by mocking the entire module, but any integration test that uses the real implementation will inherit stale state from previous tests.
notes: Files: src/ai/instructor.ts (lastRequestTime, sessionQuestionCount), src/lib/easter-eggs.ts (lastMessageIndex). Fix: export a `resetInstructorState()` and `resetEasterEggState()` function (or a single `__test_reset()` export) that resets module-level state. This is a standard pattern for testable modules with internal state.

title: setState during render anti-pattern in App reset handling
description: In app.tsx lines 70-74, the reset flag is handled by calling setState during render: `if (reset && \!resetHandled) { resetProgress(); setResetHandled(true); }`. While React technically supports this pattern for derived state, it causes an extra render cycle and is a well-known anti-pattern. It's particularly fragile because resetProgress() is a side effect (clears the persistent store) being triggered during a render pass.
notes: File: src/app.tsx lines 69-74. Fix: Move reset handling to a useEffect with reset as a dependency. This ensures the side effect runs after the initial render rather than during it: `useEffect(() => { if (reset) { resetProgress(); } }, [reset]);`. The resetHandled state can then be removed entirely.
close_reason: Reset handling moved to useEffect. Commit 60a2f07.

title: All 12 missions follow the same structure — predictable and monotonous by mid-game
description:
Every mission follows an identical pattern: dramatic print intro -> exposition print -> quiz -> print -> quiz -> command -> print closing. After 4-5 missions, the player can predict exactly what is coming next. There is no variation in step composition across missions.

Structural sameness across missions:
- Every mission opens with a dramatic-speed print step (spy flavor text)
- Every mission closes with a print step (debrief flavor text)
- Quiz and command steps always alternate with print steps
- No mission has back-to-back interactive steps
- No mission varies the difficulty curve (easy->hard or hard->easy)

Compare with effective learning games that vary their structure:
- Timed challenges for recall practice
- 'Sort these in order' for sequencing knowledge
- 'Match the concept to the description' for association
- Bonus/challenge questions for advanced learners
- Interactive scenarios ('you encounter X, what do you do?')

The current step types (print, quiz, command, ai) are too limited to create variety. Even within those types, missions never experiment with unusual combinations.
acceptance_criteria:
- This is a long-term improvement, not a quick fix
- At minimum, vary the ratio of quiz-to-command-to-print across missions
- Consider adding 1-2 new step types: ordering, matching, or scenario-based
- Later missions (8-12) should feel structurally different from early ones (1-4) to reward progression
- Even small variations (two quizzes in a row, a command step first, no closing print) would help
close_reason: Fixed — spy theme consistency + structural variety in curriculum

title: Quit confirmation says 'y/N' but any key other than y dismisses it — no 'n' handling
description: The quit confirmation dialog (app.tsx line 293-306) shows 'Quit Claude Code Academy? (y/N)'. The handler (line 154-159) checks for 'y' or 'Y' to exit, then sets confirmQuit to false for any other input. This means pressing literally any key (including accidental keypresses, arrow keys, etc.) dismisses the prompt. While the behavior is correct (N is default, so anything except Y means 'no'), the UX could be slightly improved by only dismissing on 'n', 'N', Escape, or Enter. An accidental arrow key press shouldn't silently dismiss a quit confirmation.

title: StatusBar duplicates when Enter pressed during active step

title: Mission 08: -p flag question is ambiguous — could reasonably be answered with --print
description: Mission 08 (DEEP COVER) asks 'What flag enables headless (non-interactive) mode with a direct prompt?' The expected answer is '-p'. While '--print' is in acceptedVariants, the question says 'flag' which could mean the short or long form. More critically, the explanation says '-p (or --print)' confirming both are correct, but a user who types '--print' first and gets marked wrong by the local matcher before AI evaluation would feel cheated. The local match is case-insensitive but exact, and '--print' IS in the variants list so it should work — but the question phrasing biases toward ambiguity.
acceptance_criteria:
- Question should be reworded to ask specifically for the short flag, OR converted to multiple choice
- If kept as command type, ensure both -p and --print are treated as equally correct (they already are in acceptedVariants, so this may just need a question wording tweak)

title: Debrief objectives always show green checkmarks regardless of actual per-objective results
description:
In src/screens/Debrief.tsx lines 108-117, all objectives are displayed with a green checkmark:

```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" marginBottom={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite} bold>
    OBJECTIVES:
  </Text>
  {mission.objectives.map((obj, i) => (
    <Text key={i} color={COLORS.green}>
      {"  \u2713 "}{obj}
    </Text>
  ))}
</Box>
```

Every objective unconditionally gets a green checkmark (Unicode \u2713), regardless of how the player actually performed. This is visually misleading — if a player barely scraped through with 1 star, seeing all-green checkmarks creates a dissonance with the 1-star rating shown above.

Compare with the Briefing screen (Briefing.tsx:44) which shows unchecked objectives:
```tsx
...mission.objectives.map((obj) => \`  [ ] \${obj}\`),
```

The Briefing shows empty checkboxes \`[ ]\`; the Debrief should show a mix of checked/unchecked or use a different visual treatment for the "all done" state.

**Fix:** Since the mission system doesn't currently track per-objective completion, either:
1. Change all checkmarks to a neutral indicator (e.g., a bullet or dash) to avoid implying individual objective tracking
2. Use the star rating to colorize: 3 stars = all green, 2 stars = amber, 1 star = red/amber, making the objective list reflect overall performance quality rather than pretending each was individually verified
close_reason: Debrief objectives now vary by star rating. Commit b53946c.

title: Mission 06 context window explanation says 'older information gets compressed or dropped' — misleading
description:
Mission 06 (GLASS HIGHWAY) print step says: 'When the context window fills up, older information gets compressed or dropped.'

This is misleading. Claude Code uses automatic compaction (summarization) when the context approaches its limit, but it does not arbitrarily 'drop' information in the way this text implies. The word 'dropped' suggests data loss, which could scare users into thinking their instructions or code edits might be silently lost.

A more accurate description: 'When the context window fills up, Claude Code automatically summarizes older messages to make room. Key information is preserved, but the full detail of earlier exchanges is condensed.'

The later steps in this mission correctly describe compaction as 'summarization', which makes the 'dropped' language in this step even more confusing — the mission contradicts itself.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replace 'compressed or dropped' with 'automatically summarized/compacted' or similar accurate language
- Align the terminology across all steps in Mission 06
- Avoid language that implies silent data loss
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch5 — wording, distractors, slash command quizzes

title: Mission 08 piping examples use plain text — code examples in print steps are hard to distinguish

These inline code examples are rendered in the same color and style as surrounding prose through the TypeWriter, making them hard to distinguish from explanatory text. There is no monospace/code styling, no syntax highlighting, no visual separation. A player trying to memorize these commands sees them blended into a paragraph. Consider either a dedicated 'code' step type with distinct styling, or at minimum using a different color for inline code snippets.

title: InfiniteMode quiz phase has no separator between header stats and question content
description:
In InfiniteMode quiz phase (src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx lines 364-392), the layout is:

```tsx
<Box justifyContent="space-between">
  <Text color={COLORS.cyan}>Q {currentIndex + 1}/{questions.length}</Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber}>FXP: <Text bold>{fxpEarned}</Text></Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.green}>{correct}/{total} correct</Text>
</Box>
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>
  {"────────────────────────────────────────"}
</Text>
<QuizStep ... />
```

This is the only place where a separator line appears between a stats row and interactive content. Compare with the Mission screen (src/screens/Mission.tsx) where the StatusBar at the top has NO separator line before the content area. The StatusBar just transitions directly into the step content.

Additionally, the InfiniteMode quiz stats row uses a \`justifyContent="space-between"\` horizontal layout, which is visually similar to the StatusBar but doesn't match its exact structure (StatusBar groups items in a single Box with gap={2}).

**Fix:** For consistency between InfiniteMode quizzes and Mission quizzes:
1. Either add a subtle separator after the Mission StatusBar too (to match InfiniteMode)
2. Or remove the separator in InfiniteMode (to match Mission's direct transition from stats to content)

Option 2 is simpler and reduces visual noise. The color difference between the cyan/amber stats row and the cyan question text already creates sufficient visual separation.

title: Inconsistent action prompt capitalization and formatting across screens
description:
Action prompts (the "[ENTER] do something" labels) use inconsistent capitalization, punctuation, and formatting across screens. This makes the app feel like it was built by multiple people who never compared notes.

**Capitalization inconsistencies:**

| Screen | Prompt | Style |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Logo.tsx:91 | \`> PRESS [ENTER] TO BEGIN RECRUITMENT\` | ALL CAPS, with ">" prefix |
| Onboarding.tsx:50 | \`[ENTER] to begin\` | lowercase |
| Briefing.tsx:81 | \`[ ENTER TO ACCEPT MISSION ]\` | ALL CAPS, wrapped in brackets like a header |
| Mission.tsx:192 | \`[ENTER] Restart mission\` | Title case |
| Mission.tsx:201 | \`[ENTER] continue\` | lowercase |
| Debrief.tsx:123 | \`[ENTER] Return to Mission Map\` | Title case |
| Credits.tsx:137 | \`[ENTER/ESC] Dismiss\` | Title case |
| InfiniteMode.tsx:345 | \`[ENTER] Begin assessment\` | Sentence case |
| InfiniteMode.tsx:419 | \`[ENTER] New assessment\` | Sentence case |
| MissionMap.tsx:244 | \`[UP/DOWN] Navigate  [ENTER] Start Mission\` | Title case |
| BottomBar.tsx:11 | \`[ENTER] continue\` | lowercase |

**Specific problems:**

1. **Logo** uses a unique "> PRESS [ENTER]..." format found nowhere else — it reads as a command, not a keybinding hint
2. **Briefing** wraps the entire prompt in square brackets \`[ ENTER TO ACCEPT MISSION ]\` making it look like a section header instead of an action prompt — every other prompt uses \`[KEY] action\` format
3. **BottomBar and Mission "waitEnter"** both use lowercase \`[ENTER] continue\` while Debrief uses title case \`[ENTER] Return to Mission Map\`
4. **ESC prompts** are similarly mixed: \`[ESC] Back\`, \`[ESC] Back to mission map\`, \`[ESC] menu\`

**Fix:** Standardize all action prompts to sentence case: \`[KEY] Action description\`. For example:
- \`[ENTER] Continue\`
- \`[ENTER] Begin assessment\`  
- \`[ENTER] Accept mission\`
- \`[ESC] Back to mission map\`
- \`[ESC] Menu\`

The Logo prompt can keep its dramatic phrasing but should use \`[ENTER] Begin recruitment\` format rather than the unique "> PRESS [ENTER]..." format.
close_reason: Action prompt labels standardized. Commit 14e2614.

title: BottomBar 'intel' action labeled '[H] intel' while HelpOverlay key reference says '[H] Toggle help'
description:
There is a confusing overlap between the 'H' key's meaning in different contexts:

**BottomBar.tsx line 15 (shown during missions with command steps):**
```tsx
intel: "[H] intel",
```

**HelpOverlay.tsx line 13 (global keybindings):**
```tsx
{ key: "h", label: "Toggle help" },
```

The BottomBar shows "[H] intel" as an available action during command steps (Mission.tsx line 42 pushes "intel" action when step type is "command" and phase is "step"). But pressing 'h' actually opens the help overlay (app.tsx line 188), not an intel feature. The "intel" action refers to the in-CommandStep "help" command (typing "help" into the input), not the 'h' key.

Looking at Mission.tsx's getAvailableActions:
```tsx
if (step.type === "command" && phase === "step") {
  actions.push("intel");
}
```

But the 'h' key is intercepted by app.tsx's useInput before it reaches the command input (because \`isActive\` excludes when \`isInputActive\` is true — but then overlayOpen re-enables it). Actually, during mission with input active and no overlay, the app-level 'h' handler is disabled (\`isActive: !isInputActive || overlayOpen\`). So 'h' goes to the TextInput... but the BottomBar still shows "[H] intel".

This means:
1. "[H] intel" is shown in the BottomBar when 'h' actually types 'h' into the command input
2. The actual "intel" feature is triggered by typing "help" and pressing Enter

This is a misleading UI label.

**Fix:** Either:
1. Remove the "intel" action from BottomBar during command steps, since the hint is telling the user the wrong key
2. Change the hint to \`[type "help"] Intel\` to accurately describe how to get a hint
3. Add an actual 'h' keyboard shortcut for intel during command input (would need to be handled differently since TextInput captures 'h')
close_reason: BottomBar intel removed during command steps. Commit 6f42bed.

title: InfiniteMode topic selection does not validate empty topics array from AI
description: In InfiniteMode.tsx, the topic selection phase calls generateQuiz which relies on AI quiz generation. If the AI returns an empty array of questions (which quiz-gen.ts guards against with retry logic), the infinite mode screen would transition to the quiz phase with no questions to display. While quiz-gen.ts has MAX_BATCHES retry logic, the InfiniteMode component itself does not handle the edge case where all retries fail and an empty question set is returned. The user would see an immediate 'ASSESSMENT COMPLETE' screen with 0/0 score.
notes: File: src/screens/InfiniteMode.tsx. The generateQuiz call at the generate phase could return an empty array if all AI retries fail. Add a guard: if questions.length === 0, show an error message and return to topic selection instead of proceeding to quiz phase with no content.
close_reason: InfiniteMode empty topics guard. Commit 21dd62a.

title: Logo screen ASCII art only shows 'CLAUDE' — missing 'CODE' from the product name

title: Handler input prompt uses '? ' prefix while CommandStep uses '> ' prefix
description:
Text input prompts use different prefix characters across the app:

**CommandStep.tsx lines 126-128 (answer input):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
  {">"}{" "}
</Text>
```
Uses \`>\` in amber+bold.

**Handler.tsx lines 198-199 (question input):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
  {"? "}
</Text>
```
Uses \`?\` in amber+bold.

**Logo.tsx line 91:**
```tsx
text="> PRESS [ENTER] TO BEGIN RECRUITMENT"
```
Uses \`>\` as a decorative prefix.

**NoApiKey.tsx line 67:**
```tsx
{">"} Press any key to continue without AI features
```
Uses \`>\` as a decorative prefix.

The Handler using \`?\` makes semantic sense (it's a question prompt), and CommandStep using \`>\` makes sense (it's a command prompt). However, visually the inconsistency is noticeable — both are amber+bold text input areas that look nearly identical except for the prefix character. A user switching between CommandStep and Handler sees the prompt character change for no obvious reason.

**Fix:** This is a low-severity visual note. The semantic distinction (\`>\` for commands, \`?\` for questions) is defensible. If standardizing: use \`>\` everywhere since it's the conventional terminal prompt character and appears more frequently in the app. The Handler's context (title says "HANDLER", it's in an overlay) already communicates that it's a question interface.
close_reason: Handler prefix standardized to >. Commit d7bea14.

title: Evaluator localMatch returns PARSE_FAILURE object for incorrect — misleading error response
description: In evaluator.ts (line 37-43), localMatch returns PARSE_FAILURE when no variant matches. PARSE_FAILURE has correct: false, feedback: 'Could not evaluate. Try again.', score: 0. The feedback 'Could not evaluate' is misleading — the local match did evaluate, it just found no match. This message was designed for API parse failures, not for genuine wrong answers in local mode. In the offline path, a player who types a wrong answer sees 'Could not evaluate' rather than 'Incorrect.' This is confusing. Should have a separate wrong-answer response like { correct: false, feedback: 'Incorrect.', score: 0 }.

title: Legacy mode (Konami code) CRT effect has no documentation or discoverability
description: The Konami code easter egg (MissionMap.tsx line 56-63) toggles a CRT/green-terminal aesthetic mode. This is a delightful hidden feature with scan lines, green monochrome colors, and dashed separators. However: 1) It is completely undiscoverable — there is no hint that the Konami code does anything, 2) The legacy mode state persists in save data but the label 'legacyModeUnlocked' is misleading since it is a toggle not an unlock, 3) The CRT mode text '>> LEGACY MODE ACTIVATED <<' flashes for 2 seconds and then disappears. Consider adding a subtle hint after full clearance, like 'Rumor has it there is a legacy terminal mode...' in the credits.

title: No visual rank-up ceremony when clearance level changes
description: Even if clearance level upgrades were implemented (see related issue about missing rank progression logic), there is no celebration or visual event for rank-ups. Going from RECRUIT to OPERATIVE to ELITE should be a major moment — these are the game's core progression milestones. Currently there is no screen, animation, or achievement toast for rank changes. Compare with how achievements use a slide-in toast with a terminal bell. Rank-ups deserve at least as much ceremony: a full-screen announcement, perhaps with new ASCII art or a briefing-style 'CLEARANCE UPGRADED' screen shown between the debrief and mission map.

title: HelpOverlay section titles use gray+bold while screen headers use cyan+bold
description:
The HelpOverlay (src/screens/HelpOverlay.tsx) uses a non-standard color for section subtitles:

**Line 63:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray} bold>
  {title}
</Text>
```

Section titles like "GLOBAL", "MISSION", "QUIZ STEPS", "COMMAND STEPS" render in gray+bold. But the overlay's main header uses the standard pattern:

**Line 101-103:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>
  [ KEYBINDINGS ]
</Text>
```

Every other screen uses cyan for section headers. For example:
- Credits.tsx:88,103,115 — section titles like "PROJECT", "DEVELOPED BY", "FIELD OPERATIVE" all use \`color={COLORS.cyan} bold\`
- InfiniteMode summary header uses cyan+bold

The HelpOverlay's gray section titles have lower visual hierarchy than the keybinding labels below them (\`COLORS.amber bold\`), which is inverted — the section name should be more prominent than the individual key labels.

**Fix:** Change the Section component's title Text from \`color={COLORS.gray}\` to \`color={COLORS.cyan}\` to match the Credits screen and general convention. This makes section titles visually distinct from the warmWhite label text and properly dominant over the content within each section.
close_reason: HelpOverlay headers changed to cyan+bold. Commit afa3295.

title: Onboarding screen lacks bordered container and header, visually disconnected from other screens
description:
The Onboarding screen (src/screens/Onboarding.tsx) is the only content screen that lacks both a bordered container and a section header title. Compare:

**Every other screen has a double-bordered box with a bracketed header:**
- Logo: double border, \`[ C O D E   A C A D E M Y ]\` header
- Briefing: double border, \`[ MISSION BRIEFING ]\` header  
- MissionMap: double border, \`[ MISSION SELECT ]\` header
- Debrief: double border, \`[ MISSION DEBRIEF ]\` header
- InfiniteMode: double border, \`[ DEEP COVER OPERATIONS ]\` header
- Credits: double border, \`[ DECLASSIFIED ]\` header
- NoApiKey: double border, \`[ SIGNAL INTERRUPTED ]\` header

**Onboarding has neither:**
```tsx
<Box flexDirection="column" padding={1} gap={1}>
  {/* Just TypeWriter lines and a prompt, no border, no header */}
</Box>
```

The user transitions from the Logo screen (elaborate bordered display) to Onboarding (bare text on screen) and then to MissionMap (bordered display again). The Onboarding screen feels like a rendering glitch rather than a designed screen.

**Fix:** Wrap the Onboarding content in a double-bordered container with a header like \`[ RECRUITMENT BRIEFING ]\` or \`[ INCOMING TRANSMISSION ]\` to match the visual language of every other screen. Use \`borderStyle="double"\` and \`borderColor={COLORS.cyan}\` consistent with the rest of the app.
close_reason: Onboarding bordered container merged. Commit b53946c.

title: COLORS.navy defined but never used anywhere in the codebase
description:
In src/constants.ts line 19, the COLORS object includes:

```tsx
navy: "#0D1117",
```

A search of the entire src/ directory shows zero usages of \`COLORS.navy\` outside its definition. This color is presumably intended for backgrounds, but since this is a terminal app (TUI), background colors are determined by the user's terminal theme, not by the application.

An unused palette color is a design system smell — it implies a design intention that was never implemented, or a color that was added speculatively. It creates confusion for anyone extending the palette ("should I be using navy somewhere?").

**Fix:** Remove the \`navy\` entry from COLORS. If it's intended for future use (e.g., if the app ever supports Ink's backgroundColor prop), document that intention with a comment.
close_reason: COLORS.navy removed. Commit afa3295.

title: Infinite Mode topic list uses mission index from filtered list — wrong numbering
description: In InfiniteMode.tsx (lines 93-99), the topic selection list is built by filtering MISSIONS for completed ones and then using the filter result index (idx) for numbering: 'String(idx + 1).padStart(2, "0")'. This means if missions 1, 3, and 5 are complete, they show as '01', '02', '03' instead of '01', '03', '05'. The numbering should use the original mission index from the MISSIONS array to match the mission map numbering the player is familiar with.

title: Mission 12 'worktrees' command question assumes git expertise beyond target audience
description:
Mission 12 (CONTROL TOWER) asks: 'What git feature lets you run multiple Claude Code sessions on the same repo simultaneously?' Expected answer: 'worktrees'.

The preceding print step introduces the concept: 'Use git worktrees to run parallel Claude Code sessions.' So the answer IS taught. However, the question assumes the player knows that 'worktrees' is a git-specific feature name. A developer who has never used git worktrees might answer:
- 'branches' (multiple branches, each with a Claude session)
- 'clones' (clone the repo multiple times)
- 'forks' (fork and work in parallel)

All of these are reasonable answers to 'how to run multiple sessions on the same repo' even though they are not the specific answer being sought. The concept of worktrees is advanced git knowledge that many developers never encounter.

More importantly, this is the LAST mission — the capstone. Ending on a niche git feature that many developers have never heard of is anticlimactic.
acceptance_criteria:
- Convert to multiple choice: 'worktrees', 'branches', 'submodules', 'clones'
- The preceding print step already teaches the concept, so recognition (multiple choice) is a fair test
- Recall of the specific term 'worktrees' from a single mention in one paragraph is too demanding
close_reason: Fixed in fix-hard-commands merge — converted to multiple-choice quizzes

title: Mission 08 teaches -p flag as 'headless mode' — official term is 'print mode' or 'non-interactive'
description: Mission 08 (DEEP COVER) extensively uses 'headless mode' to describe the -p/--print flag. The command step (line 758-775) asks for the 'short flag for headless mode' with expectedAnswer '-p'. The official Claude Code documentation calls this 'print mode' (hence -p/--print), not 'headless mode.' While 'headless mode' is a reasonable description of the behavior, using non-standard terminology could confuse students when they read the actual docs or '--help' output. Should either use the official term 'print mode' or explicitly note: '-p stands for print mode, sometimes called headless mode.'

title: Debrief 'FXP EARNED' label uses different color than FXP labels everywhere else
description:
FXP values are displayed in several places with inconsistent label/value color pairings:

**Debrief.tsx lines 94-98:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.green} bold>
  FXP EARNED: {fxpDisplay}
</Text>
```
Both label and value are green+bold.

**StatusBar.tsx lines 60-63:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>FXP: </Text>
<Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>{fxp}</Text>
```
Label is gray, value is amber+bold.

**MissionMap.tsx lines 126-128:**
```tsx
<Text color={primary}>
  FXP: <Text bold>{fxpFormatted}</Text>
</Text>
```
Label is cyan (primary), value is cyan+bold.

**InfiniteMode.tsx line 370-372 (quiz phase):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber}>
  FXP: <Text bold>{fxpEarned}</Text>
</Text>
```
Label is amber, value is amber+bold.

```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  FXP earned: <Text bold color={COLORS.amber}>+{fxpEarned}</Text>
</Text>
```
Label is warmWhite, value is amber+bold.

The Debrief is the outlier — it's the only place FXP uses green. Every other screen uses amber for FXP values. Since amber is the established "currency/reward" color throughout the app, Debrief should match.

**Fix:** Change Debrief.tsx FXP display to use the label/value pattern:
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
```

description:

title: Missions 01-04 are text-heavy with too many consecutive print steps
description:
The early missions front-load print steps before any interactivity. A player starting Mission 01 encounters:
- Print step (dramatic speed)
- Print step (normal speed)
- Quiz
- Print step
- Quiz
- Command
- Print step

That is 2 consecutive print steps before the first interaction. But worse, Mission 03 (BRASS COMPASS) has this pattern:
- Print step (dramatic) 
- Print step (fast)
- Quiz
- Print step
- Quiz
- Command
- Print step  <- another text dump
- Quiz
- Print step  <- closing text

And Mission 04 (PHANTOM PROTOCOL) has:
- Print (dramatic)
- Print (normal)
- Quiz
- Print (fast)
- Quiz
- Command
- Print (fast)  <- long bullet list
- Quiz
- Print

The opening double-print pattern (dramatic intro + exposition dump) repeats in every single mission. By Mission 04, the player knows to expect 'sit through two walls of text, then finally get to interact.' This predictability hurts engagement.

The ideal cadence alternates between teaching (print) and testing (quiz/command) more frequently, with shorter print steps.
acceptance_criteria:
- No more than 1 print step in a row without an interactive element (quiz or command)
- Break long exposition into shorter chunks with a question in between
- Opening dramatic print steps should be 1-2 sentences max, not full paragraphs
- Aim for a teach-test-teach-test rhythm rather than teach-teach-test-teach-test
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch3 and mission-pacing merges

title: Write curriculum.ts with all 8 missions
description:
Create src/data/curriculum.ts with all 8 missions using typed Mission/Step interfaces.

Missions with codenames:
01: FIRST CONTACT — What Is Claude Code?
02: DEAD DROP — Installation & Setup
03: BRASS COMPASS — Tools of the Trade
04: PHANTOM PROTOCOL — The CLAUDE.md File
05: SIGNAL CHAIN — Conversation Flow
06: GLASS HIGHWAY — Context Window Management
07: IRON CURTAIN — Permissions & Safety
08: DEEP COVER — Advanced Workflows

Pacing targets:
- Missions 01-02: 6-8 steps (short, player learning UI)
- Missions 03-05: 8-10 steps (standard)
- Missions 06-07: 10-12 steps (harder, multi-part)
- Mission 08: 12-15 steps (final exam, callbacks to earlier)
- Per-mission minimum 40% interactive steps (quiz or command)

Each CommandStep MUST include acceptedVariants array for no-AI matching.
Each Mission MUST include objectives array for briefing screen.

Rhythm per mission: calm(briefing) → build(teach) → test(easy quiz) → build(deeper) → test(hard quiz) → peak(command challenge) → calm(debrief) → reward(FXP)

Acceptance: MISSIONS array exports, all types satisfied, every CommandStep has 2+ variants
close_reason: Merged to main

title: Bug: Flaky test 'passes arguments to evaluateAnswer' drops first keystroke intermittently
description: In __tests__/components/CommandStep.test.tsx, the test 'passes arguments to evaluateAnswer' fails intermittently when run as part of the full suite. The submitAnswer helper writes 'ls' character-by-character with a 30ms real setTimeout between keystrokes, but the mock evaluateAnswer receives 's' instead of 'ls'. The first character 'l' is dropped because of a timing race between TextInput's value prop update and the next stdin write. When run in isolation, the test passes reliably. This is a classic TextInput controlled-component timing issue with ink-testing-library. Fix: increase the inter-keystroke delay or use a per-character flush approach.
close_reason: Fixed by replacing await delay() with await flush() in submitAnswer helper so fake timers are drained between keystrokes, preventing dropped first character.

title: Integration tests: Progress persistence round-trips
description:
Test file: `__tests__/integration/progress-roundtrip.integration.test.ts`

Module under test: `src/store/progress.ts` (integration with actual conf or memory fallback)

## Test cases

### Full lifecycle round-trip
- Fresh start -> complete mission 1 -> save -> load -> verify completedMissions includes mission-01
- Complete mission with 3 stars -> load -> verify starRatings[mission-01] === 3
- Accumulate FXP across multiple missions -> load -> verify total
- Complete all missions -> verify clearance progression opportunity

### Partial progress round-trip
- Start mission -> save step progress -> load partial progress -> verify step index
- Complete mission -> verify partial progress is cleared
- Multiple missions with independent partial progress

### Achievement round-trip
- Unlock achievement -> load -> verify in achievements array
- Duplicate unlock returns false -> load -> verify no duplicates
- Multiple achievements accumulate correctly

### Infinite mode stats round-trip
- Play session -> save result -> load -> verify stats accumulate
- Multiple sessions -> verify correct accumulation of all fields
- FXP from infinite mode adds to global FXP total

### Reset round-trip
- Complete missions -> reset -> load -> verify everything is default
- Partial progress -> reset -> verify cleared
- Achievements -> reset -> verify cleared

### Handler used flag
- markHandlerUsed -> load -> verify handlerEverUsed=true
- markHandlerUsed is idempotent

### Legacy mode toggle
- Toggle on -> load -> verify legacyModeUnlocked=true
- Toggle off -> load -> verify legacyModeUnlocked=false

### hasSaveData detection
- Default state -> hasSaveData returns false
- After completing mission -> returns true
- After earning FXP -> returns true
- After firstRunComplete -> returns true

## Important notes
- These tests should exercise the memory fallback path (mock conf to throw on construction)
- Test both the memory path and (if feasible) a temp directory conf path
- Use vi.resetModules() between tests to get fresh module state

title: Build entry point with CLI flags and exit handling
description:
src/index.tsx — the main entry point.

CLI flags: --help, --version, --reset, --no-animation
- Parse from process.argv (lightweight, no arg-parser library needed)

API key:
- Check Boolean(process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) — pass hasApiKey boolean, NOT the key string
- The SDK reads the env var directly in src/ai/client.ts
- If no key: show SIGNAL INTERRUPTED screen with setup instructions including console.anthropic.com link and cost estimate

Exit handling (CRITICAL):
- Chain waitUntilExit() on render() — without this, terminal corrupts on exit
- App must call useApp().exit() on all clean exit paths
- process.on('uncaughtException') handler that cleans up terminal state

Acceptance: All flags work, clean exit in all scenarios, no terminal corruption
close_reason: Merged to main

title: Mission onComplete captures stale fxpEarned due to React 19 batched state updates
description: In Mission.tsx line 83, the advanceStep callback calls `onComplete(computeStars(hits), fxpEarned)`. However, fxpEarned is a state variable that gets updated via `setFxpEarned` in handlers like handlePrintComplete (line 91) and handleQuizAnswer. With React 19's batched state updates, the fxpEarned value captured in the advanceStep closure can be one step behind the actual accumulated value. This means the debrief screen may show slightly fewer FXP than the player actually earned.
notes: File: src/screens/Mission.tsx lines 80-88. The advanceStep callback has fxpEarned in its dependency array (line 88) but the issue is that the last step's FXP increment and the advanceStep call happen in the same render cycle. Fix options: (1) compute final FXP from hits/steps rather than accumulated state, (2) pass the increment amount and let the parent compute, (3) use a ref to track the latest fxpEarned value.
close_reason: Stale fxpEarned fix merged. Uses refs to avoid React 19 batching closure issue. Commit d413fbe + conflict resolution 64abcd9.

title: Command step 'help' easter egg reveals answer length — trivializes the challenge
description: The CommandStep (src/components/CommandStep.tsx line 81-91) has a hidden 'help' command that reveals intel about the answer. For short answers (<= 6 chars), it reveals the length. For longer answers, it reveals the first third. For example, typing 'help' on the '/compact' question reveals 'Intel: it is a slash command, 8 characters long.' This effectively gives away the answer for most questions — there are only a handful of slash commands that are 8 characters. The first-third reveal is even more generous. This undermines the learning value. Consider making the hint less specific: topic area, general direction, or first character only.

title: Terminal title not cleared on app exit — persists in user's terminal after quitting
description: MissionMap.tsx line 35 and Briefing.tsx both call setTerminalTitle() to set spy-themed terminal titles (e.g. 'CLASSIFIED // MISSION MAP'). However, when the app exits (via 'q' > 'y' quit flow or Ctrl+C), the terminal title is never restored. The user's terminal tab/window retains the spy-themed title until they manually reset it or open a new shell. This is a minor but noticeable annoyance, especially for users who care about their terminal tab titles.
notes: File: src/app.tsx — the quit handler at line 155 calls app.exit() without clearing the title. src/lib/easter-eggs.ts line 85 has setTerminalTitle(). Fix: call setTerminalTitle('') or write the reset escape sequence (ESC ]0; BEL) before app.exit(). Also add it to the signal handlers in index.tsx.

title: README claims '8 spy-themed missions' but curriculum has 12
description: README.md line 21 states '8 spy-themed missions' but src/data/curriculum.ts defines 12 missions (mission-01 through mission-12). The README was likely written before the curriculum was expanded. This is misleading for potential users evaluating the project — they'll find 50% more content than advertised.
notes: File: README.md line 21. One-line fix: change '8' to '12'. This is separate from the Ink version issue on line 45 (tracked in cca-3f6) but could be fixed in the same PR.
close_reason: README updated to 12 missions. Commit 21dd62a.

title: MissionMap CRT mode uses dashed separators while normal mode uses solid lines
description:
In MissionMap.tsx, the CRT/legacy mode introduces a unique separator style not seen anywhere else:

**Lines 110-113 and 234-238:**
```tsx
{crt && (
  <Box justifyContent="center" marginBottom={1}>
    <Text color={dim}>
      {"- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -"}
    </Text>
  </Box>
)}
```

This is a spaced-dash separator that only appears in CRT mode. Meanwhile, the normal-mode separator on line 132 uses solid em-dashes:
```tsx
<Text color={dim}>
  {"────────────────────────────────────────"}
</Text>
```

The CRT dashed separators are also hardcoded at 40 characters wide (same width problem as cca-5b5, but a different string). The character count is 39 visible chars (20 dashes + 19 spaces).

**Fix:** For visual consistency within CRT mode, consider using a CRT-appropriate solid-line character repeated to the same width, or use the same separator logic as normal mode with just the color changed. If the dashed style is intentional for CRT retro feel, at minimum extract the separator into a shared component/constant so all separators (CRT and normal) can be updated together.
close_reason: CRT dashed separator is intentional design choice. Closed.

title: Handler and HelpOverlay use fixed pixel widths that don't adapt to terminal size
description: Two overlay components use hardcoded widths that can look wrong on different terminal sizes: (1) Handler.tsx line 154: `width={60}` — on narrow terminals (<60 cols) this will overflow or clip. On wide terminals it looks unnecessarily cramped. (2) HelpOverlay.tsx line 98: `width={44}` — same issue. The app already has useTerminalSize() available (used by TerminalGuard) and enforces MIN_WIDTH=60, but these components ignore the actual terminal width. The overlays should use a proportion of available width (e.g. Math.min(60, cols - 4)) or at minimum respect the terminal bounds.
notes: Files: src/screens/Handler.tsx line 154, src/screens/HelpOverlay.tsx line 98. The useTerminalSize hook from src/lib/terminal.tsx is available. Use it to compute width as Math.min(hardcoded_max, terminal_cols - padding). Since MIN_WIDTH is 60, the Handler's width=60 will always fit, but the UX is still suboptimal on wide terminals.
close_reason: Responsive overlay widths merged. Handler/HelpOverlay/Credits/InfiniteMode/MissionMap now use useTerminalSize(). Commit 9c41014.

title: Clearance level never upgrades from RECRUIT — no rank progression logic
description: The game tracks three clearance levels (RECRUIT, OPERATIVE, ELITE) per types.ts and shows them on the mission map and briefing screen. However, nowhere in the codebase is the clearance level ever updated from its default 'recruit' value. The saveMissionComplete function in store/progress.ts does not update clearanceLevel. Players will always see 'CLEARANCE: RECRUIT' even after completing all 12 missions. This breaks a core game mechanic described in the README ('climb from RECRUIT to OPERATIVE to ELITE'). Need to add rank-up logic — e.g., OPERATIVE after mission 4, ELITE after mission 8 or 12 — and trigger it in saveMissionComplete.
close_reason: Duplicate of cca-s92d

title: Cover blown screen has no [ESC] Menu option — player is trapped in restart-only flow
description: When cover is blown in a mission (Mission.tsx line 235-253), the screen shows 'Your identity has been compromised. Mission failed.' with only '[ENTER] Restart Mission' in the bottom bar (via getAvailableActions returning 'restart' and 'menu'). However, the ESC key handling for navigating back to mission map (app.tsx line 169) checks 'state.screen === "mission"' which is still true, so ESC should work. But the visual affordance is confusing — the bottom bar shows both 'restart' and 'menu' actions but the cover blown state intercepts Enter before the bottom bar's visual cue about ESC can be processed. The cover blown screen should more prominently show both options: '[ENTER] Restart' and '[ESC] Return to Mission Select'.
close_reason: Closed

title: AI step type has no advancement mechanism — freezes mission
description: AI steps (type: 'ai') render a placeholder <Text> with no onComplete/onAnswer callback. Phase stays 'step' forever — user cannot advance. Crashes missions 08-12 which all have AI steps. Step 9/12 of mission 09 confirmed. Either wire up a skip/continue mechanism or convert AI steps to print steps until AI feature is implemented.
close_reason: AI steps now render via TypeWriter with handlePrintComplete, advancing the mission like print steps

title: Build Briefing.tsx mission briefing screen
description:
Classified-document-style briefing before each mission.

Layout (double border with [ MISSION BRIEFING ] header):
- MISSION: ## — CODENAME
- CLEARANCE: level
- HANDLER: Instructor Haiku
- OBJECTIVES: checklist ([ ] items that check off during mission)

Acceptance: Briefing renders with all fields, objectives list correct, Enter advances

title: Mission 01 accepted variant 'npx claude' for the launch command — potentially confusing
description: Mission 01's command step (curriculum.ts line 62-69) asks 'What single command do you type to launch Claude Code?' with expectedAnswer 'claude'. The acceptedVariants include 'npx claude' which is a valid way to run it. However, this command step is asking about the interactive launch command, not the package runner. Accepting 'npx claude' here could reinforce the wrong mental model — 'claude' is the installed binary, 'npx claude' is the package runner for the game itself. This is a minor pedagogical concern.

title: Unit tests: AI evaluator module (evaluator.ts)
description:
Test file: `__tests__/ai/evaluator.test.ts`

Module under test: `src/ai/evaluator.ts`

## Test cases

### normalize (internal helper, test via evaluateAnswer behavior)
- Strips leading/trailing whitespace
- Lowercases input
- Strips prefixes: 'the ', 'a ', 'use '
- Handles empty string
- Handles string that is only a prefix (e.g., 'the')

### localMatch (internal, tested via API failure fallback)
- Returns correct=true when normalized input matches any variant
- Returns correct=false when no variant matches
- Match is case-insensitive
- Match ignores leading 'the/a/use' prefixes
- Match ignores leading/trailing whitespace

### evaluateAnswer
- Clamps user answer to MAX_INPUT (200 chars) before sending to API
- On successful API response, parses JSON and returns Evaluation object
- On API response with non-text content block, returns PARSE_FAILURE
- On malformed JSON response, returns PARSE_FAILURE
- On API error/network failure, falls back to localMatch against acceptedVariants
- Fallback localMatch returns correct=true for exact match (case-insensitive)
- Fallback localMatch returns correct=false for non-matching input
- PARSE_FAILURE has correct=false, score=0, and descriptive feedback
- Prefills assistant message with '{' for JSON steering
- Sends correct model (MODELS.EVALUATOR)

## Mocking strategy
- Mock `client.messages.create` from `../client.js`
- Provide fake responses with content blocks
- Test both success path (mocked API) and failure path (thrown error triggering localMatch)
close_reason: Merged, tests passing

title: Add context window intro lesson before teaching /compact
description: Mission 06 teaches /compact but jumps straight into it without first explaining what a context window is and why it matters. Players need foundational understanding before the tool makes sense. Add introductory steps covering: what a context window is (the 'working memory' of the conversation), why it fills up (every message/file read/tool result consumes tokens), what happens when it's full (performance degrades, older context lost), and THEN introduce /compact as the solution. Without this, /compact is just a random command with no motivation.
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch3 and mission-pacing merges

title: Build Onboarding.tsx first-run screen
description:
First-run-only welcome screen. Must complete in under 15 seconds.

Requirements:
- Check firstRunComplete in save data — skip if true
- One sentence: 'Learn Claude Code by doing.'
- Brief spy-theme intro (you've been recruited by TTD)
- Auto-advances to Mission 01 after [ENTER]
- Sets firstRunComplete=true in save data

Acceptance: Shows only on first run, skips on subsequent launches, under 15 seconds
close_reason: Merged to main

title: Mission 09: AI step about TDD is placed after TDD is fully explained — no new learning
description:
Mission 09 (DOUBLE AGENT) has an AI step asking: 'Give me an example prompt I could send to Claude Code to start a TDD workflow for adding an email validation function to a TypeScript project.'

This AI step comes AFTER a print step that fully explains the TDD workflow (5-step process) and AFTER a quiz testing whether the player knows to write tests first. By this point, the player already knows the concept. Asking them to generate an example prompt adds no new learning — it is a creativity exercise, not a knowledge check.

The same pattern appears in:
- Mission 10 AI step (write a CLAUDE.md section) — comes after CLAUDE.md conventions are fully taught
- Mission 11 AI step (security review prompt) — comes after security concepts are taught
- Mission 12 AI step (creative CI/CD use) — comes after CI/CD patterns are taught

All 4 AI steps are 'generate an example of what was just taught' — they do not introduce new concepts or test understanding in a meaningful way.
acceptance_criteria:
- When AI steps are implemented: position them to introduce or explore concepts, not just recap them
- Consider using AI steps for interactive scenarios: 'Here is a codebase situation. What would you ask Claude Code to do?' with AI evaluation of the response quality
- OR replace with more targeted quiz/command questions that test application rather than generation
close_reason: Fixed — AI step replaced with TDD prompting quiz

title: package.json repo URL says 'agent-academy' but README says 'claude-code-academy'
description: package.json (line 38) has repository URL 'git+https://github.com/mrf/agent-academy.git' and homepage/bugs URLs use 'agent-academy'. But the README (line 179) says 'git clone https://github.com/mferree/claude-code-academy.git'. The npm package name is 'claude-code-academy'. These mismatched repo URLs will cause broken links from npm, GitHub, and issue tracker URLs. Need to align all URLs to the correct repository name and GitHub username.
close_reason: Fixed all three URLs (repository, homepage, bugs) to use mferree/claude-code-academy

title: Logo screen says 'CLEARANCE LEVEL: PENDING' for returning players who already have clearance
description: The Logo screen (src/screens/Logo.tsx line 77) always shows 'CLEARANCE LEVEL: PENDING' regardless of whether the player has progress. A returning player with OPERATIVE or ELITE clearance (if rank progression were implemented) would still see 'PENDING'. For new players this is thematic, but for returning players it should show their actual clearance level or say 'CLEARANCE LEVEL: VERIFIED' to acknowledge their existing status. This is especially jarring because the game skips the logo on returning visits (skipToMap in app.tsx), but if a returning player does see the logo (e.g., after --reset or via some navigation path), it undermines their progress.

title: Dramatic speed on long print steps makes early missions painfully slow
description:
Several print steps use speed='dramatic' (40ms per character) on paragraphs that are 200-300 characters long. At 40ms/char, a 280-character paragraph takes ~11 seconds to render via typewriter. The player is just watching text appear with no interactivity.

Worst offenders:
- Mission 01 step 1: 296 chars at dramatic speed = ~12 seconds of waiting
- Mission 06 step 1: 269 chars at dramatic speed = ~11 seconds  
- Mission 07 step 1: 246 chars at dramatic speed = ~10 seconds
- Mission 08 step 1: 265 chars at dramatic speed = ~11 seconds

These are the OPENING paragraphs of missions. A player who just accepted the mission briefing (which already uses dramatic speed typewriter) has to sit through another 10+ seconds of text animation before they can do anything. This is a significant pacing problem that could cause players to abandon the game early.

Note: there appears to be no way to skip or fast-forward the typewriter animation during a print step.
design: The skip-to-complete feature is probably the biggest win here. Many terminal games let you press Space or Enter to skip text animation. This respects both the cinematic feel AND impatient players on replay. Could be implemented by checking for keypress during the TypeWriter interval and jumping charIndex to text.length.
acceptance_criteria:
- No single dramatic-speed print step should take more than 6-7 seconds to render
- Either shorten the dramatic-speed text, switch long paragraphs to 'normal' or 'fast' speed, or reserve 'dramatic' for short punchy lines (1-2 sentences max)
- Add ability to press any key to instantly complete the current typewriter animation (skip to full text)
close_reason: Fixed: TypeWriter skip on keypress, noAnimation for seen steps on cover-blown restart, dramatic→normal for long paragraphs

title: No summary or review mechanism after completing all 12 missions
description:
After completing Mission 12, the player returns to the mission map and... that is it. There is no graduation screen, no summary of what they learned, no review of their performance across all missions, and no clear 'you finished the game' moment.

The Debrief screen only shows results for the current mission. There is no equivalent for completing the entire curriculum. For a game that is themed around spy training and progression through clearance levels, the lack of a 'congratulations, you are now a fully trained operative' moment is a missed opportunity.

The save data tracks completedMissions, starRatings, total FXP, and clearanceLevel — all the data needed for a great completion screen.
acceptance_criteria:
- Add a 'Graduation' or 'Final Debrief' screen that triggers when all 12 missions are completed
- Show: total FXP earned, star ratings per mission, clearance level, total questions answered correctly
- Include a thematic sendoff message ('You have completed your training, operative...')
- Optionally: show which missions could be improved (< 3 stars) as replay motivation
- This should feel like a real achievement — the player invested significant time
close_reason: Fixed — graduation summary screen + .claudeignore question

title: No achievements screen — player has no way to view earned or available achievements
description: The game has 7 achievements (src/lib/achievements.ts) with great spy-themed names like 'Ghost Protocol', 'Dark Hours Operative', and 'The Sleeper Awakens'. Achievements are tracked in save data and shown via toast notifications when unlocked. But there is no achievements screen or list anywhere in the game where a player can: 1) See which achievements they have earned, 2) See what achievements exist to work toward, 3) Review achievement descriptions. The mission map header shows FXP and clearance but not achievement count. The Credits/Final Debrief screen shows stats but not achievements. Players need an achievements gallery accessible from the mission map (e.g., pressing 'a').
close_reason: Closed

title: E2E tests: Full app boot, mission playthrough, and achievement triggers
description:
Test file: `__tests__/e2e/full-app.e2e.test.tsx`

Module under test: The entire application from index.tsx entry point through App

## Test cases

### App boot
- App renders without crashing (no API key, --no-animation)
- NoApiKey screen appears when ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set
- Any key dismisses NoApiKey screen and shows Logo
- First-time user sees Logo -> Onboarding flow
- Returning user skips to MissionMap

### First-time user journey (no API key, --no-animation)
- Boot -> NoApiKey -> dismiss -> Logo -> ENTER -> Onboarding
- Onboarding completes -> MissionMap
- firstRunComplete is persisted
- Mission 01 is available (unlocked)
- Missions 02+ are locked

### Mission 01 playthrough (no API key, --no-animation)
- Select mission 01 from MissionMap
- Briefing screen shows correct info
- Accept mission -> Mission screen
- Walk through all steps (noAnimation makes print steps instant)
- For quiz steps: select correct answer
- For command steps: type exact expectedAnswer
- Mission completes -> Debrief
- Debrief shows stars and FXP
- Return to MissionMap -> Mission 01 shows completed

### Achievement triggers
- Complete mission with 3 stars -> PERFECTIONIST achievement fires
- Complete all missions -> FULL_CLEARANCE achievement fires
- Open handler 10 times -> HANDLERS_PET fires (requires API key mock)
- Use fake timers to simulate fast completion -> SPEEDRUNNER fires

### Keyboard routing E2E
- 'h' opens help overlay from MissionMap
- ESC closes help overlay
- 'q' from MissionMap shows quit prompt
- 'y' on quit prompt exits
- ESC from mission returns to MissionMap

### Reset flag
- Start with progress -> --reset flag -> progress cleared -> Logo shown

## Approach
- This is the heaviest test to write -- start with the boot and first-mission tests
- Use --no-animation to skip all timers and TypeWriter delays
- Mock the AI client globally (evaluateAnswer, askHandler, generateFieldAssessments)
- Use a controlled progress store (memory fallback)
- These tests exercise the REAL component tree with REAL routing logic
- Use ink-testing-library for rendering the full App component
- Simulate user input sequences for navigation

## Important notes
- E2E tests should be few but high-confidence
- Focus on critical user journeys, not exhaustive coverage
- Tag or name these files distinctly so they can be run separately (e.g., *.e2e.test.*)
- These tests will be slower -- set longer timeouts
- Must mock ALL AI calls to avoid real API requests
close_reason: 27 E2E tests covering full app lifecycle: boot, mission playthrough, achievement triggers, keyboard routing, progress persistence, star rating replays

title: Unit tests: achievements module (lib/achievements.ts)
description:
Test file: `__tests__/lib/achievements.test.ts`

Module under test: `src/lib/achievements.ts`

## Test cases

### getAchievement
- Returns Achievement for valid ID (SPEEDRUNNER, PERFECTIONIST, NIGHT_OWL, PERSISTENCE, HANDLERS_PET, GHOST_PROTOCOL, FULL_CLEARANCE)
- Returns undefined for unknown ID

- Unlocks GHOST_PROTOCOL when all missions complete AND handlerEverUsed is false
- Does NOT unlock GHOST_PROTOCOL when handlerEverUsed is true
- Returns empty array when no achievements unlock
- Returns multiple achievements when multiple conditions met simultaneously
- Does not return already-unlocked achievements (idempotency)

### checkPersistence
- Returns PERSISTENCE achievement when lastPlayedAt is 7+ days ago
- Returns null when lastPlayedAt is less than 7 days ago
- Returns null when lastPlayedAt is 0 (never played)
- Does not return if already unlocked

### checkHandlerOpen
- Returns HANDLERS_PET when handlerOpensThisSession >= 10
- Returns null when handlerOpensThisSession < 10
- Returns null for exactly 9 opens
- Returns achievement for exactly 10 opens

## Mocking strategy
- Mock `../store/progress.js` to control loadProgress() and unlockAchievement() return values
- Mock Date.now() for time-dependent tests (NIGHT_OWL, PERSISTENCE)
- Use vi.spyOn or vi.fn to track unlockAchievement calls

title: Verifying state styled differently in QuizStep vs CommandStep
description:
Both QuizStep and CommandStep show a "Verifying..." message while processing an answer, but they style it differently:

**CommandStep (src/components/CommandStep.tsx lines 145-149):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray} dimColor>
  Verifying...
</Text>
```

**QuizStep (src/components/QuizStep.tsx lines 138-141):**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.gray} dimColor>
    Verifying...
  </Text>
</Box>
```

The QuizStep wraps the "Verifying..." in a Box with marginTop={1}, giving it breathing room from the options above. The CommandStep renders it without any margin, directly after the input prompt.

Additionally, QuizStep shows the selected options in a dimmed/frozen state during the pausing phase (lines 127-143), providing visual continuity. CommandStep shows nothing between the input disappearing and the result appearing — the input field vanishes and "Verifying..." appears in its place, which is a visual jump.

**Fix:** Align both components:
1. Both should wrap "Verifying..." in a \`<Box marginTop={1}>\` for consistent spacing
2. Consider having CommandStep show the submitted answer in a dimmed state during verification (like QuizStep shows frozen options), so the user sees their input persisted
close_reason: Verifying margin consistent. Commit e2426ff.

title: PLAN.md terminology says 'Quiz = Field Assessment' but UI uses 'quiz' everywhere
description: PLAN.md defines a strict spy-theme terminology table: Quiz should be called 'Field Assessment', Hint should be 'Intel', Score should be 'Clearance Rating'. However, the in-game text uses mixed terminology. The QuizStep component shows 'Verifying...' (good spy feel), but the help overlay lists 'QUIZ STEPS' instead of 'FIELD ASSESSMENT STEPS'. The InfiniteMode screen correctly uses 'field assessment' in some places but 'quiz' internally. The help overlay (HelpOverlay.tsx line 40-43) exposes step type names directly (QUIZ STEPS, COMMAND STEPS) which breaks immersion. These should use the spy-themed equivalents consistently.

title: Bug: FULL_CLEARANCE achievement description says '8 missions' but there are 12
description: In src/lib/achievements.ts line 44, the FULL_CLEARANCE achievement description reads 'Complete all 8 missions' but the curriculum has 12 missions. The logic in checkMissionComplete correctly checks all MISSIONS.length missions, so this is a display-only bug. The description should say 'Complete all 12 missions' or better yet dynamically reference MISSIONS.length.

title: Bug: resetProgress shallow copy corrupts DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA via shared array/object references
description: In src/store/progress.ts, resetProgress() uses 'memoryStore = { ...DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA }' (line 118) which is a shallow spread. The nested arrays/objects (completedMissions, starRatings, achievements, quizStats, infiniteModeStats) are shared by reference with the module-level DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA constant. After saveMissionComplete() mutates data.completedMissions.push(), this mutation leaks back to DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA. A subsequent resetProgress() then creates a 'fresh' memoryStore that already contains the mutated arrays. The integration test at __tests__/integration/mission-flow.integration.test.tsx works around this with a cleanReset() helper that does a full deep overwrite (lines 86-103). In the memory fallback path (FUSE/WSL2 environments), this bug means progress can never be truly reset. Fix: use structuredClone(DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA) or JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(DEFAULT_SAVE_DATA)) in resetProgress().
close_reason: Closed

title: FXP earned on cover-blown restart is confusing — text says retained but behavior unclear
description:
When cover is blown in Mission.tsx, the cover-blown screen says: 'FXP earned this attempt will be retained.' But when the player presses Enter, the mission restarts with fxpEarned reset to 0 and hitsRef reset to 0 (lines 130-132 of Mission.tsx).

Looking at the code:
- On cover blown, setPhase('coverBlown') is called but fxpEarned is NOT saved anywhere
- On Enter during coverBlown phase, fxpEarnedRef.current is never persisted — it is reset to 0
- The onComplete callback (which saves to the store) is never called on cover blown

So the message 'FXP earned this attempt will be retained' appears to be a lie. The FXP is lost on restart. Either the message is wrong, or the save logic is missing.
acceptance_criteria:
- If FXP should be retained: persist fxpEarned to the save store when cover is blown, before resetting
- If FXP should NOT be retained: change the message to 'FXP from this attempt has been lost. Try again, operative.' or similar
- The on-screen text must match the actual game behavior
close_reason: Fixed — FXP resets on cover-blown restart, debrief checkmarks reflect actual performance

title: InfiniteMode unlocked check only in MissionMap — no Infinite entry from completed game state
description: Infinite Mode is shown on the mission map (MissionMap.tsx line 216-239) only when allComplete is true. But the save data has an infiniteModeUnlocked boolean (types.ts line 112) that is never set to true anywhere in the codebase. The check uses 'completedCount >= MISSIONS.length' inline instead of the persisted field. This means: 1) The infiniteModeUnlocked field in SaveData is dead code, 2) There is no notification or announcement when Infinite Mode unlocks — it just appears on the map after the last mission debrief. Should set infiniteModeUnlocked = true when all missions are complete and show a toast or announcement: 'DEEP COVER OPERATIONS UNLOCKED'.

title: Cover blown mechanic resets all progress but text dumps are unskippable — punishing loop
description:
When a player gets 3 wrong answers, their cover is blown and the mission restarts from step 0. But all print steps must be re-watched through the typewriter animation with no skip mechanism. This creates a punishing feedback loop:

1. Player reaches step 8 of 10
2. Gets 3rd wrong answer -> COVER BLOWN
3. Presses Enter to restart
4. Must re-watch steps 0-7 of text animation (potentially 60+ seconds of unskippable text)
5. Reaches the hard question again
6. If they fail again -> repeat from step 3

This is especially brutal in longer missions. Mission 08 (DEEP COVER) has 14 steps. Mission 12 (CONTROL TOWER) has 12 steps. Getting cover blown at step 11 of 14 means re-watching 8+ print step animations.

The combination of: no skip + full restart + 3-strike limit creates a frustration cliff that could cause players to quit entirely. Compare to any modern learning app (Duolingo, etc.) which either doesn't fully reset or lets you skip already-seen content.
design: The simplest immediate fix is the typewriter skip (tracked separately). The restart-from-last-unanswered idea would require tracking which steps have been seen, but it is the best UX. A middle ground: restart from the beginning but with noAnimation=true for already-seen print steps.
acceptance_criteria:
- At minimum: allow skipping typewriter animation on replay (pressing any key completes text instantly)
- Better: on cover-blown restart, skip directly to the first UNANSWERED question step, showing print steps as pre-completed
- OR: increase cover integrity from 3 to 4-5 for longer missions (8+ steps)
- The time penalty for failure should be proportional to what the player hasn't learned yet, not the total mission length
close_reason: Fixed: TypeWriter skip on keypress, noAnimation for seen steps on cover-blown restart, dramatic→normal for long paragraphs

title: No --reset confirmation prompt — flag silently wipes all progress
description: The --reset flag (src/index.tsx line 29, src/app.tsx line 72-75) immediately calls resetProgress() on mount with no confirmation dialog. A player who accidentally types '--reset' or has it in their command history will lose all progress, star ratings, FXP, and achievements with zero warning. The help text says '--reset Reset all progress (with confirmation)' but there is no confirmation implemented — it is a lie in the help text. Must add a confirmation prompt before resetting: 'WARNING: This will erase all progress. Type CONFIRM to proceed.' or at minimum a y/N prompt like the quit dialog.
close_reason: Added readline confirmation prompt before resetProgress(). User must type CONFIRM or reset is cancelled.

title: Mission 04 repeats CLAUDE.md concepts already covered — feels like filler by Mission 10
description:
CLAUDE.md is taught in Mission 04 (PHANTOM PROTOCOL), revisited in Mission 06 (GLASS HIGHWAY) as a context persistence mechanism, and then covered AGAIN in depth in Mission 10 (LEGEND BUILDER). By Mission 10, the player has answered questions about CLAUDE.md location, purpose, and what to put in it multiple times.

Specific redundancies:
- Mission 04 quiz: 'Where does the user-level CLAUDE.md file live?' -> ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
- Mission 10 command: 'Where does the user-level CLAUDE.md file live on your filesystem?' -> ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (literally the same question, different step type)
- Mission 04 teaches 'what goes in CLAUDE.md' with a bullet list
- Mission 10 teaches 'what goes in CLAUDE.md' with a nearly identical bullet list (adds project structure and versions)
- Mission 06 command: 'What file persists its instructions across context compaction?' -> CLAUDE.md

A player who got these right in Mission 04 and 06 will feel bored answering the same concepts in Mission 10. The repetition undermines the 'advancement' feeling the missions are supposed to create.
acceptance_criteria:
- Mission 10 should focus on genuinely ADVANCED CLAUDE.md topics not covered in Mission 04: custom slash commands, referencing external docs, layering strategies, real-world examples
- Remove or rephrase the duplicate ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md location question in Mission 10
- Reduce overlap between Mission 04 and Mission 10 bullet-list print steps about what to put in CLAUDE.md
- Each mission should feel like it teaches something new, not re-tests old knowledge
close_reason: Fixed — hint less revealing for short answers, Mission 10 CLAUDE.md tightened

title: Mission-06 (GLASS HIGHWAY) fails 40% interactive step ratio

title: Mission 05: /cost slash command doesn't exist — should be /context

title: Handler session cap of 20 questions has no visible counter — player hits wall unexpectedly

But the player has no visibility into how many handler uses they have remaining. The StatusBar shows '[? HANDLER]' but no count. After 20 uses the handler goes dark with a terse message. title: Mission 11 teaches .claudeignore but this file does not exist in Claude Code
description: Mission 11 (BURNED NOTICE) command step (curriculum.ts line 1203-1217) asks 'What file can you create to prevent Claude Code from reading sensitive files like .env?' with expectedAnswer '.claudeignore'. The explanation says it 'works like .gitignore but for Claude Code.' However, Claude Code does not have a .claudeignore file. The actual mechanism for controlling what Claude Code can access is through the permissions system (permissions.deny in settings.json) and .gitignore (which Claude Code respects). This is teaching players about a feature that does not exist, which could cause confusion when they try to use it in practice.
close_reason: Replaced .claudeignore command step with quiz teaching correct .gitignore mechanism. Updated README.md mission summary. Tests pass.

title: Terminal title only updates on logo and briefing screens — stale title on mission map

title: Mission 10: /init is described as analyzing the codebase but may actually be /init in Claude CLI
description:
Mission 10 says: '/init analyzes your project structure and generates an initial CLAUDE.md with detected tech stack, conventions, and project layout.'

- It may generate a CLAUDE.md interactively (asking questions) rather than fully automatically
- It may not 'detect' the tech stack — it may ask you to specify it
- The exact behavior has evolved across versions

If the description is inaccurate, a player who uses /init expecting automatic analysis will be confused when the actual behavior differs.

Additionally, the question asks 'What command initializes a new CLAUDE.md' — if the actual way to create a CLAUDE.md is simply to create the file manually (or use 'claude' and ask it to create one), then /init may not even be the best answer to teach.
acceptance_criteria:

- If /init does not exist or works differently, replace with the correct command or method

title: Build AI client with safe error wrapper
description:
src/ai/client.ts — shared Anthropic client and error handling.

Requirements:
- new Anthropic({ timeout: 15_000 }) — reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env automatically
- NEVER pass the key as a parameter or log the client object
- safeApiError() function that extracts only error.message from SDK errors:
  - 401 → 'API key appears invalid'
  - 429 → 'Rate limited. Wait a moment.'
  - 529 → 'API overloaded. Try again shortly.'
  - Network error → 'Connection lost.'
  - Timeout → 'Request timed out.'
- Never log the full error object (may contain API key in request metadata)

Acceptance: Client initializes, safeApiError handles all error types, no key leakage
close_reason: Merged to main

title: No sound effect or visual cue when cover integrity drops — only a brief red flash
description: When a player answers incorrectly and loses cover, the StatusBar (line 28-35) flashes red for 400ms. The QuizStep shows a shake animation. But there is no terminal bell, no screen flash, no 'WARNING' text overlay. Compare with the Achievement component which plays a terminal bell on unlock (line 31). A cover hit is arguably more important than an achievement unlock — it is the core risk/reward mechanic. Adding a terminal bell (\x07) on cover damage, plus perhaps a brief full-width red flash bar, would make the stakes feel more real and give stronger negative feedback.

title: Logo and NoApiKey center vertically with justifyContent but MissionMap and others don't
description:
Screens handle vertical centering inconsistently. Some center their content both horizontally and vertically, others only horizontally, and some don't center at all:

**Vertically centered (alignItems="center" + justifyContent="center"):**
- Logo.tsx:42-44 — centers both ways
- Briefing.tsx:52-54 — centers both ways  
- Debrief.tsx:68-70 — centers both ways
- NoApiKey.tsx:15-17 — centers both ways

**NOT vertically centered (top-aligned):**
- MissionMap.tsx:90 — \`padding={1}\` only, content starts at top
- InfiniteMode.tsx:243 — \`padding={1}\` only
- Credits.tsx:60 — \`padding={1}\` only
- Onboarding.tsx:39 — \`padding={1}\` only

The screens that center vertically (Logo, Briefing, Debrief, NoApiKey) tend to be short, focused screens. The ones that don't (MissionMap, InfiniteMode, Credits, Onboarding) tend to have taller content.

This is partially justifiable — long content shouldn't be vertically centered because it would look odd if it overflows. But it means when navigating from Briefing (centered) to MissionMap (top-aligned) to Briefing again, the content jumps from centered to top to centered.

**Fix:** This is a low-severity observation. The current behavior is acceptable for most cases. However, consider adding \`justifyContent="center"\` to the Credits screen at minimum — it's a short, focused screen similar to Debrief that would benefit from vertical centering. Onboarding should also center (it's only 3 lines of text).
close_reason: Vertical centering added to MissionMap/InfiniteMode. Commit 51b5c5b.

title: StatusBar and BottomBar use wrap=truncate but no other screen content does
description:
The StatusBar (src/components/StatusBar.tsx) and BottomBar (src/components/BottomBar.tsx) add \`wrap="truncate"\` to all their Text elements:

**StatusBar:** lines 57, 60, 64, 67, 72 — every Text has \`wrap="truncate"\`
**BottomBar:** lines 42, 43, 44, 50 — every Text has \`wrap="truncate"\`
**Handler:** lines 166, 184 — uses \`wrap="wrap"\` for chat messages

No other screen or component uses any wrap prop. This means:
- Mission StatusBar/BottomBar will truncate if the terminal is narrow
- But the actual mission content (quiz questions, command prompts, explanations) will wrap naturally without \`wrap="truncate"\` or \`wrap="wrap"\`
- Handler explicitly opts into wrapping for chat text
- All other screens let Ink use its default wrapping behavior

The inconsistency is that the StatusBar/BottomBar are "narrow-safe" but the content between them isn't. On a narrow terminal, the bars will truncate gracefully while the main content might wrap awkwardly.

**Fix:** This is a minor consistency note. The current behavior is reasonable — status bars should truncate to stay on one line, while content should wrap to remain readable. However:
1. Consider adding \`wrap="wrap"\` explicitly to long-form content (explanations, print steps) for clarity of intent
2. The Handler correctly uses \`wrap="wrap"\` — other screens with long text (Briefing intel text, Credits descriptions) should do the same for consistency
close_reason: Wrap=truncate on status bars is intentional. Commit 51b5c5b.

title: Credits screen 'self-destruct' animation fires automatically — no way to cancel or skip
description: The Credits screen (src/screens/Credits.tsx) has three phases: summary -> credits -> destruct -> wipe. When entering the 'destruct' phase, a countdown automatically starts (3, 2, 1...) and then a red screen wipe fires. The ESC key is only handled during the 'destruct' phase (line 40-42), not during the 'wipe' phase. If the player misses the ESC window during the 3-second countdown, the red screen wipe runs to completion and closes the credits. This is a fun theatrical touch but could frustrate players who want to re-read the credits text. There is also no way to go back from 'credits' to 'summary' phase — Enter/ESC only moves forward.

- Returns true when full Konami sequence entered: up up down down left right left right b a
- Returns false during partial sequence
- Returns false for incorrect key in middle of sequence
- Resets position on unrecognized key (e.g., character 'x')
- Resets after successful completion (can be triggered again)
- Maps upArrow key correctly to 'up'
- Maps downArrow key correctly to 'down'
- Maps leftArrow key correctly to 'left'
- Maps rightArrow key correctly to 'right'
- Maps 'b' character input correctly
- Maps 'a' character input correctly
- Returns null for unmapped keys (e.g., 'c', 'z', tab)
- Resets when sequence broken at position > 0 but first element matches restart (e.g., up up up should be at position 1, not 0)

### getLoadingMessage
- Returns a string from the LOADING_MESSAGES array
- Never returns the same message twice in a row (consecutive uniqueness)
- Returns a string (not undefined) even after many calls

### setTerminalTitle
- Writes correct escape sequence to stdout
- Includes the title parameter in the escape sequence

## Notes
- For getLoadingMessage, test by calling it many times (e.g., 100) and verifying no two consecutive returns are equal
- For setTerminalTitle, mock process.stdout.write and verify the escape code format
- The Konami tracker tests are purely synchronous -- no async needed
close_reason: Merged, tests passing

title: Mission map locked missions show no tooltip or reason — player does not know why they are locked
description: On the mission map, locked missions show '[ ] XX CODENAME  LOCKED' in dim text. When a player selects a locked mission and presses Enter, nothing happens — the key press is silently ignored (MissionMap.tsx line 80: only navigates if isMissionUnlocked returns true). There is no feedback explaining why the mission is locked or what needs to be done to unlock it. Should show a message like 'Complete Mission XX to unlock this mission' when attempting to enter a locked mission, or show the prerequisite below the locked mission title.

title: Screen tests: Handler overlay
description:

Module under test: `src/screens/Handler.tsx`
