title: Mission 03 lists 'additional tools like Agent' but never teaches Agent tool in any mission
description: Mission 03 (BRASS COMPASS, curriculum.ts line 164) says: 'There are additional tools like Agent (for spawning subagents) and others you will encounter later.' The debrief (line 233) repeats: 'you will meet additional tools like Agent in later missions.' But no later mission dedicates a step or quiz to teaching the Agent tool specifically. Mission 08 (DEEP COVER) mentions subagents briefly in a print step and has one quiz about their benefit, but never covers the Agent tool by name or teaches how to invoke it. The promise of 'you will encounter later' is broken. Either add Agent tool coverage to Mission 08 or soften the promise.
close_reason: Added command step to Mission 08 that explicitly quizzes learners on the Agent tool name, fulfilling Mission 03's promise that they'll meet Agent in later missions.

title: No progress indicator for new players — unclear how many missions total or how far along
description: A new player starting the game sees missions appearing one at a time as they complete them (sequential unlocking). But there is no visible indicator of total progress: 'Mission 3 of 12', '25% complete', or similar. The mission map shows all 12 missions listed but locked ones just say 'LOCKED' with no total count visible. The bottom bar during missions shows 'STEP X/Y' for the current mission but nothing about overall game progress. The StatusBar shows mission number but not out of how many. Players should have visibility into how much of the curriculum remains — it helps with motivation and time planning.
close_reason: Closed

title: SPEEDRUNNER achievement threshold (2 minutes) is very easy — most missions are short
description: The SPEEDRUNNER achievement (achievements.ts line 12-15) triggers on completing a mission in under 2 minutes. Given that most missions have 6-8 steps of quiz/print content, and quiz answers can be submitted in seconds, completing a mission in under 2 minutes is trivial — especially for someone replaying. The achievement is called 'Speed of Light' which implies exceptional speed, but any competent first-time player will earn it on most missions. Consider either tightening the threshold (e.g., 60 seconds) or making it cumulative ('Complete 5 missions in under 2 minutes each').

title: Mission 07 teaches 'allowedTools' as legacy but permissions.allow as preferred — needs clearer deprecation note
description: Mission 07 (curriculum.ts line 664) mentions: 'The legacy allowedTools key still works but permissions.allow/ask/deny are more expressive and preferred.' This is mentioned once in passing within a dense print step. For a tutorial, this legacy vs. modern distinction needs more emphasis. A player who later encounters 'allowedTools' in existing configs may be confused. Consider a quiz question that specifically tests: 'You see allowedTools in a project config. What is the modern equivalent?' to reinforce the migration path.

title: Briefing screen spoils the first print step of every mission
description:
The Briefing screen (Briefing.tsx) extracts the first print step's text and displays it as 'INTEL' in the mission briefing. This means when the player accepts the mission and enters the actual gameplay, the first thing they see is the exact same text they just read on the briefing screen. This is repetitive and deflating — it feels like the game is wasting your time.

Example: Mission 01's briefing shows the full 'Welcome to the Terminal Training Division...' paragraph, and then the mission starts with that same paragraph again via the typewriter effect.

This affects ALL 12 missions since every mission starts with a print step.
design: Simplest fix: add an optional 'briefing' field to the Mission type. If present, use that for INTEL. If absent, generate a short summary or use the mission title + objectives only. This avoids changing the step flow.
acceptance_criteria:
- Briefing INTEL should either show a unique briefing blurb (separate field on the Mission type) OR show a condensed/different version of the intro
- Alternatively, skip the first print step during mission playback if it was already shown in the briefing
- The player should never read the same paragraph twice back-to-back
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch2 merge

title: MissionMap uses empty Box spacers instead of marginTop for vertical spacing
description:
The MissionMap screen (src/screens/MissionMap.tsx) uses empty \`<Box marginTop={1} />\` elements as spacers in three places:

**Line 135:**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} />
```
Between the separator line and the mission list.

**Line 205:**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} />
```
Between the last mission and the infinite mode entry.

**Line 241:**
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} />
```
Between CRT separator and the footer help text.

This is an anti-pattern in Ink layouts. Every other screen in the codebase uses \`marginTop={1}\` or \`marginBottom={1}\` on the actual content elements, or uses \`gap={1}\` on the parent container. Empty Box spacers:

1. Add unnecessary elements to the component tree
2. Are harder to reason about when debugging layout issues
3. Don't match the pattern used in every other screen

**Fix:** Replace empty Box spacers with marginTop/marginBottom on adjacent elements. For example, line 135 could be removed if the mission list's first item gets \`marginTop={1}\`, or the separator gets \`marginBottom={1}\`.
close_reason: Empty spacer Boxes replaced with marginTop. Commit 51b5c5b.

title: BottomBar shows '[ANY] Skip' for print steps but TypeWriter requires specific keypress
description: The BottomBar (src/components/BottomBar.tsx) shows '[ANY] Skip' as an action label for print steps (defined in ACTION_LABELS line 13). This corresponds to the TypeWriter component (src/components/TypeWriter.tsx line 35) which does indeed advance on any keypress. However, once the typewriter completes, the phase changes to 'waitEnter' and the bottom bar shows '[ENTER] Continue'. The transition from '[ANY] Skip' to '[ENTER] Continue' is jarring — the player presses a key to skip animation, then has to specifically press Enter. It would be more consistent to either always require Enter (skip to end of text on Enter, advance on Enter again) or always allow any key.
close_reason: Not a bug: TypeWriter's useInput(() => setCharIndex(text.length), { isActive: animating }) fires on ANY keypress during animation, which matches the [ANY] Skip label exactly.

title: ESC hint text inconsistent: some dim gray, some with brackets, some without
description:
The "[ESC]" dismiss/back hints are styled inconsistently across overlays and screens:

**Handler.tsx line 210-212:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>
  {isStreaming ? "ESC to cancel" : "ESC to close"}
</Text>
```
No brackets around ESC. Lowercase "to cancel" / "to close".

**HelpOverlay.tsx line 121:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>Press ESC or h to close</Text>
```
No brackets. Adds "Press" prefix. Mentions alternative key.

**InfiniteMode.tsx line 286:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>[ESC] Back to mission map</Text>
```
Has brackets. Sentence case.

**InfiniteMode.tsx line 305, 347:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.gray}>[ESC] Back</Text>
```
Has brackets. Single word.

The overlays (Handler, HelpOverlay) use bare "ESC" without brackets, while non-overlay screens use \`[ESC]\` with brackets. This is particularly confusing because the overlays ARE where ESC is most meaningful.

**Fix:** Standardize all ESC hints to use the bracketed format consistent with other keybinding hints: \`[ESC] Close\`, \`[ESC] Cancel\`, \`[ESC] Back\`. The HelpOverlay can mention the 'h' alternative as \`[ESC/H] Close\`. Drop the "Press" prefix — no other prompt uses it.
close_reason: ESC hints standardized. Commit afa3295.

title: Mission 01 next-mission teaser says 'we get it installed' — breaks if player already has it installed
description: Mission 01 (curriculum.ts line 75) closing text says: 'Next mission: we get it installed on your machine.' This assumes the player has not installed Claude Code yet. But someone playing Claude Code Academy likely already has Claude Code installed (they may even be running the game inside Claude Code). The teaser should be more inclusive: 'Next mission: the installation and authentication protocol.' This avoids the assumption that installation hasn't happened yet.

title: Mission 10 /init explanation says 'scans your codebase' — actual /init is interactive, not automatic
description: Mission 10 (LEGEND BUILDER, curriculum.ts line 1037-1038) says: 'The /init command analyzes your project -- tech stack, directory structure, conventions -- and generates an initial CLAUDE.md automatically.' The command step explanation (line 1097) says: '/init scans your codebase -- tech stack, directory structure, conventions -- and generates a CLAUDE.md as a starting point you can refine.' In practice, /init is an interactive command that prompts the user through the process, not a fully automatic scan-and-generate. The mission text makes it sound like a one-shot magic command. Should clarify that /init guides you through setting up a CLAUDE.md interactively.
close_reason: Updated three locations in Mission 10 curriculum.ts to accurately describe /init as an interactive guided process rather than an automatic scan-and-generate command.

Manual setup (no create-ink-app — it generates stale CJS boilerplate).

title: Credits screen uses manual space indentation instead of paddingLeft or marginLeft
description:
The Credits screen (src/screens/Credits.tsx) indents content lines using string literal spaces \`{"  "}\`:

**Lines 92-125 (throughout the screen):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  {"  "}Claude Code Academy
</Text>
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  {"  "}Terminal Training Division
</Text>
```

This pattern is used for every content line under a section header (PROJECT, DEVELOPED BY, FIELD OPERATIVE). Eight lines total use \`{"  "}\` (2-space indent).

Compare with the Briefing screen which also indents content under labels:

**Briefing.tsx lines 43-47:**
```tsx
...mission.objectives.map((obj) => \`  [ ] \${obj}\`),
```

The Briefing uses inline string spaces too, but as part of a template literal that constructs a single text block for TypeWriter. The Credits screen is rendering individual Text elements — it could use Ink's Box with marginLeft or paddingLeft instead of string spaces.

Using string spaces for indentation is fragile (changing indent width requires updating every line) and doesn't scale with terminal width.

**Fix:** Wrap indented content in a Box with \`marginLeft={2}\` or \`paddingLeft={2}\`:
```tsx
<Box marginTop={1} flexDirection="column">
  <Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>PROJECT</Text>
  <Box marginLeft={2} flexDirection="column">
    <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>Claude Code Academy</Text>
    <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>Terminal Training Division</Text>
    <Text color={COLORS.warmWhite} dimColor>"Learn by doing. Survive by knowing."</Text>
  </Box>
</Box>
```
close_reason: Credits indentation uses marginLeft. Commit afa3295.

title: QuizStep and CommandStep say '[Enter] to continue' with lowercase — inconsistent with rest of UI
description: The QuizStep (line 173) and CommandStep (line 273) both show '[Enter] to continue' after revealing results. Every other action prompt in the game uses uppercase formatting: '[ENTER] Begin', '[ENTER] Accept mission', '[ENTER] Return to mission map', '[ENTER] Continue', '[ENTER] Restart Mission'. The lowercase '[Enter] to continue' breaks visual consistency. Should be '[ENTER] Continue' to match the established pattern.

title: Commander step self-assess mode shows expected answer before asking — defeats the purpose
description: When there is no API key, the CommandStep self-assessment mode (src/components/CommandStep.tsx line 238-258) shows: 'Your answer: [user input]' then 'Expected: [correct answer]' then 'Did your answer match? [Y/N]'. Showing the expected answer before asking the player to self-assess means they can just press Y regardless of their actual answer. The self-assessment is supposed to be an honor system fallback, but by showing the expected answer it becomes a 'do you want credit? Y/N' prompt. Consider showing only the player's answer first, asking Y/N, and only revealing the expected answer after they respond.

title: Mission 06 repeats /clear command step — same question already asked in Mission 05
description: Mission 06 (GLASS HIGHWAY) line 538-552 asks 'What slash command clears your conversation entirely to start fresh?' with expectedAnswer '/clear'. This exact same question was already asked in Mission 05 (SIGNAL CHAIN) line 400-414. Both missions have identical command steps asking for /clear. The Mission 06 occurrence adds no new learning — by that point the player has already been tested on /clear. This step should be replaced with a command step testing something unique to Mission 06's context window management topic, such as asking about /context or asking what file type persists through compaction.

title: README has TODO placeholders for demo GIF, screenshots — looks unfinished to visitors
description: The README (lines 40-41, 53-54, 110-111, 125-126) contains HTML comments with TODO placeholders: '<\!-- TODO: Replace with actual GIF recording of gameplay -->', '<\!-- TODO: Replace with screenshot of mission map -->', etc. While these are hidden comments not rendered on GitHub, they indicate that the README's visual storytelling is incomplete. For an open-source launch, a product like this needs at minimum: 1) A demo GIF showing gameplay, 2) A screenshot of the mission map, 3) A screenshot of a quiz in action. The README is text-heavy and would benefit enormously from visual proof of what the game looks like.

title: Achievement component fires terminal bell even when noAnimation is true
description: In Achievement.tsx, the terminal bell (\x07) is written to stdout when an achievement is shown. The component respects noAnimation for the slide-in/slide-out animation timing, but the bell fires regardless. In automated testing or CI environments where noAnimation is true, the bell is unnecessary noise. In the vitest environment it's harmless (stdout is captured), but in any scenario where noAnimation means 'quiet mode' the bell is unexpected.
notes: File: src/components/Achievement.tsx. Check if the bell write is inside a useEffect. If noAnimation is true, skip the bell. This is a minor polish item — the bell is part of the spy-themed aesthetic but should respect the quiet mode flag.
close_reason: Achievement bell guarded by noAnimation. Commit 2336bd7.

title: Mission number formatting differs between Briefing and StatusBar
description:
The mission number is formatted differently depending on where it appears:

**Briefing.tsx line 16 + line 39:**
```tsx
const missionNumber = mission.id.replace("mission-", "");
// ...
\`MISSION:     ${missionNumber} — ${mission.codename}\`,
```
Uses \`replace("mission-", "")\` — for "mission-01", this produces "01" (preserves leading zero).

**Mission.tsx line 74:**
```tsx
const missionNumber = parseInt(mission.id.replace(/\\D/g, ""), 10) || 1;
```
Uses parseInt which strips leading zeros — "mission-01" produces \`1\`.

**StatusBar.tsx line 47:**
```tsx
const missionNum = String(missionNumber).padStart(2, "0");
```
Re-pads to 2 digits — converts \`1\` back to "01".

**MissionMap.tsx line 146:**
```tsx
const num = String(i + 1).padStart(2, "0");
```
Uses array index + 1, zero-padded.

So the pipeline for mission-01 is:
- Briefing: "01" (string replace, naturally zero-padded)
- Mission -> StatusBar: parseInt gives 1 -> padStart gives "01" (round-trip through number)
- MissionMap: index 0 + 1 = 1 -> padStart gives "01"

While the final display is consistent (all show "01"), the Briefing.tsx approach is fragile — if a mission ID were ever "mission-1" instead of "mission-01", Briefing would show "1" while StatusBar would show "01".

**Fix:** Extract mission number formatting to a shared utility function:
```tsx
function formatMissionNumber(missionId: string): string {
  const num = parseInt(missionId.replace(/\\D/g, ""), 10) || 1;
  return String(num).padStart(2, "0");
}
```
Use it in Briefing, Mission, StatusBar, and MissionMap for guaranteed consistency.
close_reason: Mission number format standardized. Commit afa3295.

title: BottomBar shows [ESC] Menu during missions but ESC key is unreachable in active gameplay
description: The BottomBar always includes '[ESC] Menu' during mission gameplay (getAvailableActions in Mission.tsx line 67). However, the app-level useInput in app.tsx (line 151) has isActive: \!isInputActive || overlayOpen, where isInputActive = (screen === 'mission' && \!overlayOpen). During active mission steps, isInputActive is true, so \!isInputActive is false and overlayOpen is false, making the entire app-level useInput inactive. The ESC handler (line 163-173) that navigates from mission to missionMap cannot fire. The player is shown [ESC] Menu but pressing ESC does nothing during quiz, command, print, or waitEnter phases. The only way to exit a mission is to complete it or get cover blown. This is a P2 UX issue because the UI promises an action that doesn't work.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integration tests: Mission gameplay flow (print -> quiz -> command -> debrief)
description:
Test file: `__tests__/integration/mission-gameplay.integration.test.tsx`

Module under test: Mission screen + QuizStep + CommandStep + StatusBar + BottomBar working together

## Test cases

- Advance through print step (wait for TypeWriter, press ENTER)
- Answer quiz correctly (select correct option)

title: Mission 07: 'allowedTools' answer conflicts with question about 'auto-approve'
description:
Mission 07 (IRON CURTAIN) has this quiz question: 'What settings key lets you auto-approve specific tools?' The correct answer is 'allowedTools' (option B, index 1).

The question asks about 'auto-approve' but the answer is 'allowedTools'. This is correct, but the distractor option 'autoApprove' (option A, index 0) looks extremely plausible — it is literally the phrase from the question converted to camelCase. A reasonable learner might pick 'autoApprove' thinking it is the more intuitive key name.

This is a well-constructed distractor in one sense, but it is arguably unfair because:
1. The preceding print step says 'You can configure allowedTools in your settings' — teaching the concept
2. But the question rephrases it as 'auto-approve' which maps more naturally to 'autoApprove'
3. A careful reader will get it right, but a skimmer will get burned by the question's own phrasing

The question is technically fair but the phrasing creates unnecessary confusion. Either match the terminology from the teaching step or acknowledge the trick.
acceptance_criteria:
- Rephrase the question to use the same terminology as the teaching text: 'What settings key lists tools that run without prompting?'
- OR keep the question but improve the explanation to explicitly address why 'autoApprove' is wrong: 'The key is allowedTools, not autoApprove. It lists which tools skip permission prompts.'
- Questions should test understanding of the concept, not ability to avoid paraphrase traps
close_reason: Fixed in curriculum-batch3 and mission-pacing merges

title: Define types.ts and constants.ts

Create src/types.ts with discriminated union for all step types:

- CommandStep must include expectedAnswer: string AND acceptedVariants: string[]

Create src/constants.ts with:
- Model IDs (HANDLER, GENERATOR, EVALUATOR) — single source of truth
- Color palette hex values (warm white #E8E6E3, amber #FFB627, green #39FF14, red #FF3131, cyan #00D4FF, gray #6B6B6B, gold #FFD700)
- Timing values (typewriter speeds, pause durations, transition durations)
- Version string

Acceptance: Types compile, constants importable from any module

title: Mission 12 closing text says 'Final debrief' but game has no final debrief screen for last mission
description: Mission 12 (CONTROL TOWER, curriculum.ts line 1345-1348) says: 'Final debrief, operative. You have completed the full Claude Code training program.' But this is just a print step within the normal mission flow. After the mission completes, the player sees the standard debrief screen (stars, FXP, cover integrity) — the same one shown for every other mission. There is no special 'Final Debrief' or 'Graduation' screen. The Credits screen exists but is only accessible from the mission map via [C]. The completion of the last mission should feel special — perhaps auto-navigating to credits, showing a unique debrief variant, or displaying a congratulatory message.
close_reason: After Mission 12 debrief, ENTER now navigates to the Credits screen (which already has a FINAL DEBRIEF summary phase) instead of the mission map. Continue prompt updated to say 'View final debrief'.

title: Briefing screen typewriter uses 'dramatic' speed for multi-line content — painfully slow
description: The Briefing screen (src/screens/Briefing.tsx line 69) uses speed='dramatic' (40ms per character) for the entire briefing text, which includes the mission number, clearance level, handler, objectives list, and intel paragraph. At 40ms per character, a typical briefing of ~200 characters takes 8 seconds to display. Players who replay missions or move quickly through the game will find this agonizing. The briefing has structured data (labels like MISSION, CLEARANCE, HANDLER) that should appear instantly, with only the INTEL section using typewriter effect. At minimum, the speed should be 'fast' or 'normal' rather than 'dramatic'.
close_reason: Structured header now renders as static text instantly; TypeWriter only animates INTEL paragraph

title: PLAN.md lists only 8 mission codenames but game has 12 missions
description: PLAN.md (lines 57-66) lists mission codenames 01-08 only. Missions 09-12 (DOUBLE AGENT, LEGEND BUILDER, BURNED NOTICE, CONTROL TOWER) are not listed in the plan document. This is a documentation drift issue — the plan was written before missions 9-12 were added but was never updated. Not user-facing but could confuse contributors reading the plan.

title: Mission 06 has 12 steps including 4 print+command — longest mission with no pacing variation
description: Mission 06 (GLASS HIGHWAY) has 12 steps — the most of any mission. The step sequence is: print, print, print, print, quiz, print, quiz, command, print, quiz, print, command, quiz, command, print. That is 7 print steps and 5 assessment steps. Four print steps in a row at the start creates a wall of text before any interactivity. By contrast, Mission 01 opens with a print then immediately follows with a quiz. Missions should ideally alternate between teaching (print) and testing (quiz/command) more frequently. Consider breaking Mission 06 into two missions or interspersing the opening prints with quiz steps.

title: NoApiKey screen fires onContinue on every single keypress instead of once
description: In NoApiKey.tsx line 9, the useInput handler calls onContinue() on every keypress with no guard. If the parent component's onContinue handler is slow (e.g. triggers a state update and re-render), multiple rapid keypresses can cause the callback to fire multiple times. The screen says 'Press any key to continue' implying a single action, but the handler fires continuously. This can cause double-transitions or flickering if the user happens to press multiple keys.
notes: File: src/screens/NoApiKey.tsx lines 8-11. Fix: add a local state guard (e.g. `const [dismissed, setDismissed] = useState(false)`) and check it before calling onContinue. Set it to true on first call.
close_reason: NoApiKey double-fire fix merged. Added dismissed guard. Commit a60b988.

title: Bug: Clearance level never upgrades from 'recruit' — progression logic missing
description: The SaveData type includes 'clearanceLevel: ClearanceLevel' with possible values 'recruit', 'operative', 'elite'. This is displayed in both the MissionMap (line 89) and Briefing screen (line 36). However, there is no code anywhere in the codebase that ever updates clearanceLevel from its default value of 'recruit'. The mock-progress helper createCompletedSaveData() manually sets clearanceLevel to 'elite', confirming the intent for it to progress. The expected behavior would be something like: recruit (0-4 missions), operative (5-8 missions), elite (9-12 missions). This is a missing feature — the clearanceLevel field exists in the data model and is displayed in the UI, but the progression logic was never implemented.

I can help with anything related to your current mission.' would set context, establish the character, and match the Briefing screen which lists 'HANDLER: Instructor Haiku'. title: Bug: Handler overlay '[?]' shown in BottomBar but unreachable during active mission steps
description: In src/app.tsx, the app-level useInput handler (line 200) is gated by '{ isActive: \!isInputActive || overlayOpen }', where isInputActive is 'state.screen === mission && \!overlayOpen'. During normal mission gameplay (quiz or command step with no overlay), isInputActive=true, making the app handler INACTIVE. This means the '?' key to open the handler overlay never reaches the app's input handler during the exact time when the player would need help. The BottomBar (via getAvailableActions) correctly shows '[?] Handler' as available when hasApiKey is true, but the key binding is unreachable. The handler can only be opened by first opening the help overlay (h) and then pressing '?', which is a non-obvious workaround. The e2e test '__tests__/e2e/full-app.e2e.test.tsx' line 497-509 explicitly tests and documents this limitation. Fix: either add a '?' handler to the Mission component that bubbles up to app, or restructure the keyboard routing so '?' reaches the app handler during mission steps.

title: Logo screen wraps Text around TypeWriter creating nested Text components
description:
In the Logo screen (src/screens/Logo.tsx), TypeWriter is wrapped inside Text components to apply color:

**Lines 55-61:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.warmWhite}>
  <TypeWriter text={ASCII_LOGO} speed="dramatic" onComplete={advancePhase} />
</Text>
```

**Lines 65-70:**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>
  <TypeWriter text="[ C O D E   A C A D E M Y ]" ... />
</Text>
```

Since TypeWriter internally returns \`<Text>{text.slice(0, charIndex)}</Text>\`, this creates nested \`<Text><Text>...\</Text></Text>\`. In Ink, nested Text components work (inner inherits outer styles), but it means:

1. The outer Text sets color/bold, the inner Text from TypeWriter has no explicit styles — this works because of Ink's inheritance, but it's fragile
2. The dimColor Text wrapper on lines 77-83 wraps TypeWriter in \`<Text dimColor>\` — dimColor is applied to the outer Text but the inner Text from TypeWriter renders raw text that inherits dimColor correctly

This is not currently broken but is an Ink antipattern. If TypeWriter ever adds its own color prop (as suggested in cca-4ri), the nested Text color could conflict.

Additionally, this pattern is inconsistent — Briefing.tsx:70 wraps TypeWriter in Text for color, but Onboarding.tsx and Mission.tsx do NOT wrap TypeWriter in a colored Text.

**Fix:** This is best addressed alongside cca-4ri (TypeWriter color prop). Once TypeWriter accepts a color prop, remove the outer Text wrappers and pass color directly: \`<TypeWriter text={...} color={COLORS.warmWhite} />\`
close_reason: Logo nesting fixed. Commit 8d99efd.

title: Result feedback spacing differs between QuizStep and CommandStep
description:
After answering, the result display (CONFIRMED/COMPROMISED + explanation) has different spacing in QuizStep vs CommandStep:

**QuizStep result (src/components/QuizStep.tsx lines 146-183):**
- Shows CONFIRMED/COMPROMISED
- On wrong: shows all options with correct one highlighted, wrapped in \`marginTop={1}\`
- Shows explanation in \`<Box marginTop={1}>\` — separated from the answers by a blank line

**CommandStep result (src/components/CommandStep.tsx lines 151-172):**
- Shows CONFIRMED/COMPROMISED
- On wrong: shows expected answer on the next line (no margin)
- Shows explanation with no marginTop — directly following the expected answer

The QuizStep explanation has a full line of breathing room (\`marginTop={1}\`) between the answer review and the explanation text. The CommandStep crams the explanation right against the expected answer with no gap. This makes the CommandStep feel more cramped.

**Fix:** Add \`marginTop={1}\` before the explanation text in CommandStep's result phase to match QuizStep's spacing. Wrap the CommandStep result in a structure that mirrors QuizStep:

```tsx
{phase === "result" && (
  <Box flexDirection="column">
    {correct ? (
      <Text color={COLORS.green} bold>CONFIRMED</Text>
    ) : (
      // ...COMPROMISED + expected answer
    )}
    <Box marginTop={1}>
      <Text color={COLORS.gray}>{step.explanation}</Text>
    </Box>
  </Box>
)}
```
close_reason: Feedback spacing consistent. Commit e2426ff.

title: Briefing screen shows 'HANDLER: Instructor Haiku' — ties to specific model, will date poorly
description: The Briefing screen (src/screens/Briefing.tsx line 37) hardcodes 'HANDLER: Instructor Haiku' in every mission briefing. This references the Claude Haiku model powering the handler. While it is a fun in-universe name, it creates two problems: 1) If the handler model changes (from Haiku to a newer model), the name becomes inaccurate, 2) It mixes product knowledge (model names) with the game fiction in a potentially confusing way. A new player may think 'Haiku' is a character name, while a savvy player recognizes it as the model name. Consider either committing fully to the fiction ('HANDLER: Agent Whisper') or the transparency ('HANDLER: Claude Haiku').
close_reason: Changed 'HANDLER: Instructor Haiku' to 'HANDLER: Agent Whisper' in src/screens/Briefing.tsx:37

title: Mission 04 calls user 'operative' but clearance level is still RECRUIT at that point
description: Mission 04 (PHANTOM PROTOCOL) closes with 'Protocol established, operative.' But at mission 4, the player is still at RECRUIT clearance (even if rank progression were implemented, 4 out of 12 missions would likely still be RECRUIT tier). Missions 01-03 correctly use 'recruit' in their closing text. Mission 05 uses no rank term. Mission 08 calls the player 'operative' ('Basic training is over'). This inconsistency breaks the progression fantasy. The closing text for each mission should use the player's current rank at minimum, or avoid rank terms entirely until the player has actually earned that rank. Affected missions: 04 (line 321), 07 (line 697), 08 (line 719), 09 (line 975), 10 (line 1101), 11 (line 1220), 12 (line 1346).

title: Mission 11 .env question is generic security knowledge, not Claude Code specific
description:
Mission 11 (BURNED NOTICE) asks: 'What file should you add to .gitignore to prevent accidentally committing secrets?' Expected answer: '.env'.

This is a general best practice for any development workflow. It has nothing specifically to do with Claude Code. A player taking a Claude Code training course would expect questions about Claude Code, not general git hygiene.

The question could be made Claude Code-specific: 'What type of file should you never ask Claude Code to read or include in CLAUDE.md because it contains secrets?' — still teaches .env awareness but in the context of Claude Code usage.

Similarly, the 'API keys' command question in the same mission is generic security knowledge rather than Claude Code-specific. The mission would be stronger if its security questions centered on Claude Code-specific attack vectors and defenses.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reframe the .env question to be Claude Code specific: focus on what happens when Claude Code reads a .env file, or how to configure Claude Code to skip .env files
- Consider replacing with a question about Claude Code's specific security features or settings
- General security knowledge is good to reinforce but should be connected to the specific tool being taught
close_reason: Fixed — graduation summary screen + .claudeignore question

title: Mission 02 teaches curl install but also says 'no Node.js needed' — contradicts npx usage
description: Mission 02 (DEAD DROP) teaches that Claude Code installs via 'curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash' and says 'no external runtime required.' This is correct for the Claude Code binary itself. However, the game is run via 'npx claude-code-academy' which requires Node.js. Additionally, there was a previous issue (cca-2zd3) noting this npm-based install contradiction. The README also says 'node >= 20' is required. More importantly, the mission quiz question says the native installer requires 'None — it installs with zero dependencies' but this ignores that some users may use npm to install Claude Code. The mission should mention that there are multiple installation methods — the native installer (curl) and npm — and clarify which requires what.
close_reason: Updated Mission 02 to clarify both installation methods (curl native installer and npm), fixed quiz questions and print text to remove false 'zero dependencies' claim and acknowledge Node.js requirement for npm path

title: Debrief continue prompt uses justifyContent flex-end while all others use center or natural flow
description:
In src/screens/Debrief.tsx line 121, the continue prompt uses:

```tsx
<Box justifyContent="flex-end" marginTop={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    [ENTER] Return to Mission Map
  </Text>
</Box>
```

This right-aligns the continue prompt. The same pattern in Briefing.tsx line 79 also uses \`justifyContent="flex-end"\`:

```tsx
<Box justifyContent="flex-end" marginTop={1}>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>
    [ ENTER TO ACCEPT MISSION ]
  </Text>
</Box>
```

But every other screen places action prompts in natural left-aligned flow:
- Mission.tsx:199 — no justifyContent (left-aligned)
- Onboarding.tsx:49 — no justifyContent (left-aligned)
- InfiniteMode.tsx:343,417 — no justifyContent (left-aligned)
- Credits.tsx:136 — justifyContent="center"
- MissionMap.tsx:243 — no justifyContent (left-aligned)

The Briefing and Debrief screens right-align their prompts while nothing else does. This creates a visual inconsistency where the user has to look in different places for the "what do I press next" hint.

**Fix:** Change both Briefing and Debrief to use left-aligned or center-aligned prompts to match the convention used everywhere else. Since these screens are centered containers, removing \`justifyContent="flex-end"\` (defaulting to left alignment within the bordered box) would match the majority of screens.
close_reason: Debrief prompt alignment centered. Commit 51b5c5b.

title: Debrief objective checkmarks are computed by formula, not actual performance
description:
In Debrief.tsx (line 121), objective completion is computed as:
  const passed = stars === 3 || i < Math.ceil(mission.objectives.length * (stars / 3));

This means objective checkmarks are purely cosmetic — they are derived from the star count using a formula, not from actual player performance on specific objectives. A mission with 3 objectives and 2 stars will always show the first 2 objectives as passed and the third as failed, regardless of which questions the player actually got wrong.

Example: Mission 07 (IRON CURTAIN) has objectives:
1. Understand Claude Code's permission system
2. Know the different permission modes  
3. Configure tool permissions in settings
4. Understand when to use dangerous overrides

If a player aces questions about #3 and #4 but misses questions about #1, the debrief will still show objectives 1-3 as passed (because the formula counts from the top). This is misleading.

This matters because objectives are a key feedback mechanism — players look at them to understand WHAT they need to study more.
design: Adding a mapping from steps to objectives would require an 'objectiveIndex' field on quiz/command steps. That is more work but produces genuinely useful feedback. The simpler fix is to just show all objectives as a reference list without pass/fail marks, and let the star rating carry the performance signal.
acceptance_criteria:
- Map quiz/command steps to specific objectives so the debrief shows which objectives were actually met vs missed
- OR remove objective checkmarks entirely if mapping is too complex, and just show the star rating + FXP
- Fake checkmarks are worse than no checkmarks — they undermine trust in the feedback system
close_reason: Fixed — FXP resets on cover-blown restart, debrief checkmarks reflect actual performance

title: Handler overlay header says '[ HANDLER ]' but keybinding label says '[? HANDLER]'
description:
The Handler feature is referenced with inconsistent labels:

**StatusBar.tsx line 73 (visible during missions):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.amber} wrap="truncate">
  [? HANDLER]
</Text>
```
Shows as \`[? HANDLER]\` — key + label combined.

**Handler.tsx line 158 (overlay header):**
```tsx
<Text color={COLORS.cyan} bold>
  [ HANDLER ]
</Text>
```
Shows as \`[ HANDLER ]\` — no key reference.

**BottomBar.tsx line 12:**
```tsx
handler: "[?] handler",
```
Shows as \`[?] handler\` — lowercase.

**HelpOverlay.tsx line 19:**
```tsx
{ key: "?", label: "Ask handler" },
```
Shows as \`[?] Ask handler\` — verb phrase.

Four different representations of the same feature:
1. \`[? HANDLER]\` — StatusBar
2. \`[ HANDLER ]\` — overlay header
3. \`[?] handler\` — BottomBar
4. \`[?] Ask handler\` — HelpOverlay

**Fix:** Standardize:
- Overlay header: \`[ HANDLER ]\` (fine as-is, matches other headers)
- StatusBar shortcut: \`[?] Handler\` (match BottomBar format with title case)  
- BottomBar: \`[?] Handler\` (title case to match other actions)
- HelpOverlay: \`[?] Handler\` or keep \`Ask handler\` since HelpOverlay uses verb phrases
close_reason: Handler header standardized. Commit afa3295.

title: Build help overlay with keybinding reference
description:
Keybinding reference overlay accessible via h key.

Content (changes based on context):
- Global: h=help, q=quit
- In mission: ?=handler, ESC=menu, ENTER=continue, UP/DOWN=navigate
- In quiz: UP/DOWN=select, ENTER=confirm

Styled as a centered modal with single border.

title: Command step expects exact string match for 'deniedTools' — casing sensitivity could frustrate
description:
Mission 07 (IRON CURTAIN) command step expects 'deniedTools' as the answer to 'What settings key blocks specific tools from being used entirely?'

The accepted variants are: 'deniedTools', 'denied tools', 'deniedTools array', 'the deniedTools key'. 

The local matcher is case-insensitive (CommandStep converts to lowercase), so 'deniedtools' would match 'deniedtools'. But the camelCase nature of 'deniedTools' means the player needs to know the exact casing convention to feel confident. They might type:
- 'denied_tools' (snake_case habit) — NOT accepted
- 'DeniedTools' (PascalCase) — would match via case-insensitive comparison
- 'denied-tools' (kebab-case) — NOT accepted
- 'blockedTools' (synonym) — NOT accepted
- 'denyTools' — NOT accepted

The AI evaluator would likely handle these, but the question is testing recall of a specific camelCase JSON key name, which is more about memorization than understanding.
acceptance_criteria:
- Convert to multiple choice: 'deniedTools', 'blockedTools', 'restrictedTools', 'disabledTools'
- OR add more variants: 'denied_tools', 'denied-tools', 'blocked tools', 'deny tools'
- JSON key names are better tested via recognition than recall
close_reason: Fixed — expanded acceptedVariants across all command steps with casing coverage

title: Achievement notification uses gold border but amber name — inconsistent emphasis hierarchy
description:
The Achievement component (src/components/Achievement.tsx lines 107-122) uses a mixed gold/amber color scheme:

```tsx
<Box borderStyle="double" borderColor={COLORS.gold} ...>
  <Text color={COLORS.gold} bold>ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED</Text>
  <Text color={COLORS.amber} bold>{name}</Text>
  {description && <Text color={COLORS.gray}>{description}</Text>}
</Box>
```

The hierarchy is:
1. Border: gold
2. Title "ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED": gold
3. Achievement name: amber
4. Description: gray

COLORS.gold (#FFD700) and COLORS.amber (#FFB627) are visually very similar — gold is slightly more yellow, amber slightly more orange. Using both in a small 3-line notification creates a subtle color shift that reads as "almost the same but slightly off" rather than a deliberate design choice.

Compare with every other screen where the border and header use the same color (cyan border + cyan header is the standard).

**Fix:** Use a single color for the achievement's prominent elements. Either:
1. All gold: change the name to \`color={COLORS.gold}\` to match the border and title
2. All amber: change the border and title to \`color={COLORS.amber}\` 

Option 1 (all gold) is slightly better since gold is the "special/premium" color already associated with stars, and achievements are premium rewards.
close_reason: Achievement colors consistent. Commit e2426ff.

title: Improve localMatch fuzzy tolerance
description: Make localMatch more forgiving: strip backticks, quotes, trailing periods, leading dollar-sign prompts, collapse multiple spaces, normalize whitespace. Benefits all users since localMatch is the API-failure fallback too.
close_reason: Fixed — localMatch now strips backticks, quotes, $, trailing periods, collapses whitespace

title: Write missions 9-12 with content quality pipeline
description:
Four new missions, AI-generated with human review.

Missions:
09: WIRE TAP — Claude Code in CI/CD (GitHub Actions, scripts)
10: DEEP VEIN — Multi-file refactors (agent orchestration patterns)
11: SIGNAL BRIDGE — MCP Server setup (writing and connecting servers)
12: DOUBLE AGENT — Claude Code + Git workflows (commit messages, PR review)

Content quality pipeline:
1. Agent generates static lesson steps
2. Agent generates 3-4 field assessments with explanations
3. Agent generates 1-2 command challenges with variant lists
4. Human reviews for: factual accuracy, distractor quality, difficulty calibration
5. Add to MISSIONS array
6. Playtest for pacing

Quality rubric per quiz:
- One clearly correct answer
- Three plausible distractors
- Explanation that teaches (not just confirms)
- Difficulty matches mission position

Acceptance: 4 new missions pass quality review and playtest

title: Spy theme is inconsistent — some missions lean in, others barely reference it
description:
The spy theme is strongest in mission names (codenames like IRON CURTAIN, BURNED NOTICE, DOUBLE AGENT) and opening/closing print steps ('operative', 'field training', 'cover blown'). But the middle of each mission — where the actual teaching happens — reads like a standard tutorial with no thematic flavor.

Examples of theme inconsistency:
- Mission 03 opens with 'Every operative needs to know their tools' but then teaches Read/Edit/Write/Bash/Glob/Grep with zero spy metaphors. The tools could be called 'surveillance', 'forgery', 'interception' etc. in the spy world.
- Mission 06 uses 'intelligence channel' and 'bandwidth limits' in the intro but then drops into generic technical language for the rest.
- Mission 09 is called 'DOUBLE AGENT' (evocative!) but the content is about multi-file edits and git — there's nothing 'double agent' about it.
- Quiz questions and explanations are entirely theme-free — they read like documentation.

The theme works best when it is a light touch (Mission 11 'BURNED NOTICE' about security is thematically perfect). It feels forced when a dramatic spy intro leads into 'The Edit tool uses str_replace to swap specific text in a file.'
acceptance_criteria:
- This is a polish/enhancement pass, not a rewrite
- Add light thematic flavor to 3-4 quiz explanations per mission (e.g., 'Good intel, agent.' instead of just the explanation)
- Ensure codenames have at least a loose thematic connection to the mission content
- Consider renaming Mission 09 codename to something that maps to 'coordinating across multiple targets' rather than 'double agent'
- Do NOT over-theme the technical content — the spy flavor should enhance, not obscure, the learning
close_reason: Fixed — spy theme consistency + structural variety in curriculum

title: Handler shows only last 4 messages of history — no scroll, previous context lost
description: The Handler overlay (src/screens/Handler.tsx line 147) shows only the last 4 messages via 'history.slice(-4)'. The conversation history is sent to the API (up to 8 messages via HISTORY_WINDOW in instructor.ts), but the UI only shows the most recent 4. If a player has a multi-turn conversation, earlier questions and answers scroll off-screen permanently. There is no scroll mechanism in Ink, but the handler could at minimum show more messages (8-10) or provide a 'show more' affordance. Alternatively, showing a count like '(3 earlier messages)' would acknowledge the hidden history.
close_reason: Increased visible history from 4 to 8 messages. Added '(N earlier messages)' indicator when history exceeds the visible window. VISIBLE_MESSAGES constant at module scope.

title: MissionMap FXP calculation uses star-only formula — ignores actual FXP earned in missions
description: In MissionMap.tsx (line 299-301), the missionFxp function calculates FXP as 'stars * 100 + 40'. This is a display-only formula used in the star rating row, not the actual FXP earned during the mission. The actual FXP earned depends on how many quiz/print steps the player completed (10 per correct answer, 5 per print step read). So the mission map shows a different FXP number than what was actually earned. This is confusing — a player who earned 85 FXP in a mission but got 3 stars will see '340 FXP' next to their stars on the map. These numbers should either match or the map should not show per-mission FXP.
close_reason: Duplicate of cca-4m21

title: README says '7 spy-themed achievements' but only 7 are defined — number is correct but fragile

Consider either removing the specific count ('spy-themed achievements to unlock') or generating it. Low priority — just noting for future-proofing.

title: No indication of which missions have been replayed — star upgrades are invisible
description: When a player replays a completed mission and gets a better star rating, saveMissionComplete (store/progress.ts line 93-96) updates the star rating only if the new one is higher. But there is no visual indication on the mission map that a mission has been replayed or that the star rating improved. The player just sees the new star count without any 'improved\!' badge or animation. The FXP also stacks on each replay (line 98) which could lead to infinite FXP farming by replaying easy missions. Consider either: 1) Not awarding FXP on replays, 2) Only awarding the delta FXP, or 3) Showing a 'REPLAY' or 'IMPROVED' indicator.

title: Mission 10 quiz about user vs project CLAUDE.md priority uses 'in context' — vague answer
description: Mission 10 quiz (curriculum.ts line 1023-1034) asks what happens when user-level says 'use Jest' but project-level says 'use Vitest'. The correct answer is 'Both are loaded -- project-level instructions take precedence in context.' The explanation says 'Claude Code treats them as additive, with project context naturally taking precedence for project work.' This is somewhat vague and hand-wavy. The actual behavior is that both CLAUDE.md files are loaded into the system prompt simultaneously, and Claude resolves any conflicts based on specificity. The answer could be more precise: 'Both are loaded; the more specific project-level instruction wins for that project.'

title: Mission complete flow does not update quiz stats — quizStats in SaveData always 0/0
description: SaveData has a quizStats field (types.ts line 91-94) tracking {correct, total}. But saveMissionComplete (store/progress.ts line 82-104) never updates quizStats. The quiz statistics from each mission (how many quiz/command steps were answered correctly vs. total) are not tracked in persistent storage. The only stat that persists is FXP and star ratings. This means there is no aggregate tracking of quiz performance across missions, which would be valuable for showing improvement over time or in the final debrief/credits screen.
close_reason: saveMissionComplete now accepts quizCorrect/quizTotal and accumulates into SaveData.quizStats. Caller in app.tsx computes totals from non-print step count and correct count from wrongAnswers.length.

title: loadProgress() called during render body in App component — should be memoized or moved to effect
description: In app.tsx line 41, `loadProgress()` is called directly in the render body of the App component. This means it runs on every single re-render, reading from the conf store (or memory fallback) each time. While the operation is synchronous and fast, it's an anti-pattern in React — side-effectful reads during render can cause issues with concurrent features and make the component harder to reason about. The result is only used to check `firstRunComplete` for initial routing.
notes: File: src/app.tsx line 41. Fix options: (1) Use useMemo to memoize the initial load, (2) Move to a useState initializer: `const [initialProgress] = useState(() => loadProgress())`, (3) Read only the specific field needed. Option 2 is simplest and most idiomatic.
close_reason: loadProgress moved to useState initializer. Commit 60a2f07.

title: Hardcoded hex color '#1a7a0a' in MissionMap legacy mode
description:
In `src/screens/MissionMap.tsx` line 87, the CRT/legacy mode defines a dim green color as a raw hex literal:

```tsx
const dim = crt ? "#1a7a0a" : COLORS.gray;
```

This is the only hardcoded hex color outside `src/constants.ts`. If the green palette value changes, this dim variant won't track with it.

**Fix:** Add a `dimGreen` (or `crtDim`) entry to the COLORS constant in `src/constants.ts` and reference it here. Alternatively, since this is a one-off CRT easter egg, at minimum extract it to a named constant at the top of the file.
close_reason: Added COLORS.dimGreen. Commit d7bea14.

title: Mission 08 says 'This is the final exam' but there are 4 more missions after it
description: Mission 08 (DEEP COVER, curriculum.ts line 719) says: 'You have completed basic training. Now we go deep cover -- advanced workflows that separate field operatives from headquarters analysts.' Then: 'Headless mode, piping, hooks, subagents, and multi-session orchestration. This is the final exam.' But Mission 08 is only the 8th of 12 missions. Calling it 'the final exam' is misleading — there are 4 more missions covering multi-file ops, custom instructions, security, and production patterns. This text was likely written when the game only had 8 missions (matching the PLAN.md). Should say something like 'This is your advanced certification exam' instead.
close_reason: Changed 'This is the final exam.' to 'This is your advanced certification exam.' in curriculum.ts line 718

title: StatusBar cover integrity colors jump from green to red at 1 — no amber/warning state at 2

This means cover goes from green (3 or 2 remaining) directly to red (1 remaining). There is no amber/warning state at 2 remaining. Given that the game's color language defines amber as the player's attention color, showing amber at 2 remaining would give a valuable early warning before the critical red at 1. The progression should be: 3 = green, 2 = amber, 1 = red.

title: Build pre-publish security and smoke test checklist
description:
Automated pre-publish verification.

Checklist (scripted):
1. Clean build: rm -rf dist/ && npx tsup
2. npm audit --audit-level=moderate
3. npm pack --dry-run (inspect for unexpected files)
4. Secret scan: grep dist/ for sk-ant, AKIA, password, secret, token
5. License check: npx license-checker --summary
6. Smoke test with AI: node dist/index.js (play mission 1)
7. Smoke test without AI: unset ANTHROPIC_API_KEY && node dist/index.js
8. Smoke test invalid key: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-invalid node dist/index.js
9. Test npx flow: npm pack && npx ./claude-code-academy-*.tgz
10. Verify clean install: npm ci

Acceptance: All 10 checks pass, script exits non-zero on any failure
close_reason: Merged to main

title: Update permissions lesson to cover newer permissions.allow/ask/deny format
notes:
IMPORTANT NUANCE TO COVER:

1. Rule evaluation order: deny → ask → allow. First match wins, so deny always takes precedence.

2. allowedTools vs permissions.allow express the same concept (pre-approve without prompting), but permissions lets you express all three states in one block. With just allowedTools you'd need a separate disallowedTools key for denials.

3. Subtle gotcha: allowedTools does NOT constrain bypassPermissions mode. It only pre-approves listed tools — unlisted tools fall through to the permission mode. So with bypassPermissions, your allowedTools list is effectively a no-op for security.

4. Recommendation: Prefer permissions.allow/ask/deny in settings.json for clarity. Use --allowedTools CLI flag for throwaway sessions. Legacy allowedTools top-level key still works but less expressive.
close_reason: Fixed — tool count, /init description, permissions format updated

title: Nine test files duplicate the tick() helper instead of importing from render-ink.ts
description: The tick() helper function (which advances fake timers by a given number of milliseconds) is defined in __tests__/helpers/render-ink.ts and exported, but nine test files redefine it locally with identical implementations: ScreenTransition.test.tsx, Achievement.test.tsx, StatusBar.test.tsx, TypeWriter.test.tsx, mission-flow.integration.test.tsx, Briefing.test.tsx, Debrief.test.tsx, Onboarding.test.tsx, and Mission.test.tsx. This duplication means if the tick() implementation needs to change (e.g. to handle React 19 scheduler quirks differently), it must be updated in 10 places instead of 1.
notes: Files with duplicate tick(): __tests__/components/ScreenTransition.test.tsx:18, Achievement.test.tsx:21, StatusBar.test.tsx:9, TypeWriter.test.tsx:17, __tests__/integration/mission-flow.integration.test.tsx:104, __tests__/screens/Briefing.test.tsx:27, Debrief.test.tsx:47, Onboarding.test.tsx:51, Mission.test.tsx:77. The canonical version is in __tests__/helpers/render-ink.ts. Fix: delete the local definitions and add tick to the import from render-ink.ts in each file.
close_reason: Deduplicated tick() across 9 test files. Commit 2336bd7.

title: Help hint in CommandStep reveals answer length and prefix — too generous for short answers
description:
The CommandStep component has a 'help' feature (type 'help' to get a hint). The getHelpHint function reveals:
- For answers <= 3 chars: the exact length ('the answer is X characters long')
- For answers > 3 chars: the first third of the answer ('the answer starts with ...')

For short answers this is essentially giving away the answer:
- 'claude' (6 chars) -> reveals 'cl...' (only 4 chars left to guess)
- '/cost' (5 chars) -> reveals '/c...' (the slash + first letter narrows it to very few options)
- '/clear' (6 chars) -> reveals '/c...' (same as /cost — actually ambiguous between /cost, /clear, /compact, /config!)
- 'Edit' (4 chars) -> reveals 'Ed...' (trivially completable)
- '-p' (2 chars) -> reveals 'the answer is 2 characters long' (narrows significantly)

For longer answers it is more helpful without being a giveaway. But for the common short answers in this curriculum, the help system either gives too much away or creates confusing ambiguity (multiple /c... commands).
acceptance_criteria:
- For answers <= 6 chars: show only the character count, not the prefix
- For answers > 6 chars: current behavior (show first third) is fine  
- Consider adding a second level of help that shows more, so players can progressively request hints
- The hint for slash commands should perhaps say 'It is a slash command' rather than revealing the first character
close_reason: Fixed — hint less revealing for short answers, Mission 10 CLAUDE.md tightened

title: Wire progress persistence with conf
description:
Save/load game state using conf package.

Schema (versioned for future migration):
{
  schemaVersion: 1,
  completedMissions: string[],
  starRatings: Record<string, 1|2|3>,
  fxp: number,
  clearanceLevel: 'recruit'|'operative'|'elite',
  achievements: string[],
  quizStats: { correct: number, total: number },
  infiniteModeUnlocked: boolean,
  firstRunComplete: boolean,
}

Requirements:
- Use conf's migrations option for schema versioning
- Wrap all conf calls in try/catch with fallback to in-memory state (handles FUSE/networked filesystem failures on WSL2)
- Auto-save after each step completion (not just mission completion)
- Save partial progress: if user exits mid-mission, save current step index
- On return: 'Resume Mission 03 from step 4? (Y/n)' prompt
- --reset flag with confirmation prompt: 'This will erase all progress. Continue? (y/N)'

Acceptance: Progress persists between sessions, partial save/resume works, --reset works with confirmation
close_reason: Merged to main

title: InfiniteMode generating phase blocks all input — no way to cancel or escape
description: In InfiniteMode.tsx, the useInput hook on line 177 is gated by { isActive: phase \!== 'generating' }, which suppresses ALL keyboard input during the question generation API call. Since startGeneration() does not use an AbortController, the user is completely stuck until the API call either succeeds, fails, or times out (which could be 15+ seconds). There is no way to press ESC to abort generation or return to the mission map.
close_reason: Added ESC escape hatch during generating phase with AbortController signal threading

title: AI step type renders as plain TypeWriter text with no visual distinction
description: In Mission.tsx (line 212-218), the 'ai' step type is rendered identically to a print step — just a TypeWriter showing step.prompt. There is no visual cue that this is supposed to be an AI-generated response or interactive element. The step.prompt for Mission 10's AI step is a writing exercise ('Write a short example CLAUDE.md section...') and Mission 11's is a prompt-crafting exercise. But since these render as plain text, the player just reads the prompt and presses Enter to continue. The AI step has no input mechanism, no evaluation, no feedback. It looks and behaves exactly like a print step. Either these steps need actual AI interaction or they should be clearly labeled as reflection prompts.
close_reason: Duplicate of cca-6046 (same root cause: AI steps are non-functional)

title: Debrief 'REVIEW — WRONG ANSWERS' section numbering starts at 1 regardless of question position
description: The Debrief screen (src/screens/Debrief.tsx line 129-142) shows wrong answers with sequential numbering (1, 2, 3...). But this numbering is relative to the wrong answers list, not the mission step index. A player who got questions 3 and 7 wrong sees them numbered as 1 and 2, with no reference to where in the mission they occurred. Adding 'Step X:' or 'Question X of Y:' context would help players know which part of the mission they struggled with for replay purposes.
